0.label: polite address term
0.description: Short deferential responses or phrases containing 'sir' or 'madam' used as respectful forms of address in dialogue

1.label: CH/PH digraph in speaker labels
1.description: The feature activates on the 'CH' or 'PH' character sequence, particularly at the start of speaker/character names in dramatic texts (SCHOOLMASTER, EPILOGUE, CHIEF JUSTICE, PHOEBE, CHAMBERLAIN)

2.label: Latin/Greek -us suffix
2.description: Tokens that are the 'us' ending of Latin or Greek derived proper nouns and words (e.g. Proteus, Cyprus, Hesperus, Coriolanus, Theseus)

3.label: ROSENCRANTZ or similar mid-token split
3.description: Feature activates on the interior letters of the name ROSENCRANTZ (specifically 'EN') and similar mid-word positions containing 'v' sounds or syllable boundaries in polysyllabic proper nouns and words, particularly where a consonant cluster or vowel follows a consonant mid-token

4.label: adversative conjunction at turn boundary
4.description: The word 'But' appearing at the start of a new speaker's turn or immediately after a short acknowledgment/response, marking a contrastive or redirecting pivot in dialogue

5.label: quoted word or phrase internal token
5.description: Tokens occurring inside quotation marks, typically mid-word or continuation tokens within a directly quoted word or short phrase

6.label: heart
6.description: The word 'heart' used in emotional, metaphorical, or figurative contexts across Shakespeare's works

7.label: substring 'ad' or 'om' in word-internal position
7.description: The feature fires on tokens that are fragments of longer words where 'ad' or similar letter clusters appear mid-word, specifically detecting the subword token 'ad' as it appears inside words like 'ballad', 'academe', 'plead', 'entombed'

8.label: past participle ending in -ed
8.description: Tokens that are past participles (or past tense verb forms) ending in '-ed', often appearing in passive constructions or verb phrases

9.label: Elided 'the' apostrophe
9.description: The feature fires on the apostrophe in elided/contracted forms of 'the' such as th' (th'effect, th'occurrents, th'interpretation, th'rest, th'adoption, th'present) or mid-word apostrophes representing elided vowels in Shakespearean verse contractions

10.label: reflexive possessive 'own'
10.description: The feature activates on the word 'own' when used as a reflexive or emphatic possessive modifier (e.g., 'his own', 'our own', 'thine own', 'mine own'), indicating self-referential possession or agency.

11.label: fa- prefix
11.description: The feature activates on tokens beginning with the letters 'fa' (such as 'fausse', 'faces', 'fap', 'fairest', 'favors', 'faites', 'fawned', 'faction', 'fairer')

12.label: word starting with 'o' after apostrophe or standalone
12.description: The feature fires on tokens that are the letter 'o' at the start of words like 'owed', 'owe', 'o'er', 'obedient', 'o'' (of), 'o'erflowing' - specifically the initial 'o' character in Shakespearean words beginning with 'o', often following possessive or contracted forms

13.label: blank line / verse paragraph break
13.description: The feature fires on the newline or whitespace token that separates verse lines or speech turns in Shakespearean dramatic verse, specifically the opening guillemet-wrapped whitespace/newline token marking the start of a new line or speaker turn

14.label: am/amb sequence
14.description: Tokens beginning with 'am' including the word 'am' and words starting with 'amb' (ambitious, ample, amen, ambition)

15.label: I had/I'll make construction
15.description: First-person past subjunctive or volitional verb phrase, typically 'I had rather', 'I had as lief', 'I had thought', or similar idiomatic expressions following a sentence boundary

16.label: love
16.description: The feature detects the word 'love' (or occasionally similar emotional/relational terms), firing specifically on that token across varied grammatical contexts (noun, verb, infinitive)

17.label: imperative Let/Let's
17.description: The feature fires on the word 'Let' (or 'Let's') when used as an imperative or hortative construction, introducing a command or exhortation directed at one or more people

18.label: past participle verb forms with 'wa-'/'mo-'/'deser-'/'perfor-'/'vis-' prefixes
18.description: The feature activates on the first syllable or early characters of past participle or past tense verb forms, particularly those beginning with 'wa', 'mo', 'deser', 'perfor', or 'vis' that follow auxiliary verbs or are used as predicates

19.label: speaker change newline
19.description: Whitespace or newline token immediately following a closing guillemet or speech-end punctuation, marking the boundary between a character's dialogue and the next speaker label in a Shakespeare play script

20.label: preposition after 'you'
20.description: The feature fires on prepositions (between, with, against, upon, unto, at) that immediately follow the word 'you'

21.label: preposition following noun/verb
21.description: The feature fires on the prepositions 'of' and 'with' when they appear after a noun or verb in a prepositional phrase, typically mid-clause in poetic or literary prose

22.label: speaker label after line break
22.description: The feature fires on newline/whitespace tokens that immediately precede a character's speech tag (speaker name) in dramatic dialogue, marking the transition point between one character's speech and the next speaker's label.

23.label: bl- onset
23.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'bl' (the start of words like 'blush', 'blaze', 'blue', 'blast', 'blade', 'blow', etc.)

24.label: newline/paragraph break after sentence ending
24.description: Whitespace or line break token immediately following a period that ends a complete sentence, marking a speaker turn or verse line boundary

25.label: first-person polite response verb
25.description: Verbs (thank, bring, help, go with) that immediately follow a first-person pronoun (I or I'll) in polite or courteous speech acts directed at another character

26.label: MAR* character cue
26.description: Fires on the token 'MAR' at the start of a character name label (MARINA, MARGARET, MARIA) indicating a speech prefix in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue

27.label: verb inflection -ed/-s
27.description: Tokens that are inflected forms of motion or growth verbs (specifically 'walk', 'grow', 'stay' with -ed or -s suffixes), capturing a morphological pattern of present-tense or past-tense verb endings on a small set of semantically related verbs

28.label: Capital M after space
28.description: The feature fires on the token 'M' when it is the first letter of a proper noun (name or title) beginning with capital M, typically appearing after a space or punctuation in dialogue or narrative contexts

29.label: ld/rd cluster
29.description: Tokens containing the consonant cluster 'ld', 'bd', or similar liquid+stop sequences, often mid-word or suffix positions

30.label: letter j
30.description: The feature activates on the letter 'j' (or 'J'), whether at the start, middle, or end of a word, across various lexical and syntactic contexts

31.label: The at line/clause start
31.description: The word 'The' (capitalized) appearing at the beginning of a new line or major clause boundary, typically after a line break in verse, introducing a new syntactic unit mid-passage

32.label: line break after punctuation
32.description: Feature activates on whitespace/newline characters that follow end-of-line punctuation or occur at verse line boundaries in Shakespeare's poetic text

33.label: speaker change newline
33.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or line break token that immediately follows a period or exclamation mark ending one character's speech and precedes a new speaker tag in a dramatic dialogue

34.label: re substring
34.description: Fires on the letters 're' appearing mid-word, particularly where 're' follows a consonant or vowel cluster as part of a larger word (e.g., 'quarrelsome', 'rebels', 'interpreter', 'threefold', 'secrets', 'treason', 'preparation', 'apprehension', 'threes', 'prevent', 'rewarded')

35.label: 'in' after pause/conjunction
35.description: The word 'in' appearing after a comma or conjunction (but, and, yet, marry) that creates a brief pause, typically introducing a prepositional phrase expressing manner, opinion, or circumstance

36.label: past counterfactual 'had'
36.description: Tokens of 'had' used in counterfactual, subjunctive, or hypothetical past constructions (e.g. 'I had rather', 'would there had been', 'had he died')

37.label: object pronoun after verb
37.description: Third-person or second-person object pronouns (him, it, you) appearing immediately after a verb, functioning as the direct object of that verb

38.label: first-person pronoun 'I' after clause boundary or emphasis
38.description: The feature fires on the token 'I' (first-person singular pronoun) when it appears after a clause boundary, subordinate clause, or emphatic/contrastive construction, often mid-sentence or after an interjection

39.label: Speaker label after dialogue
39.description: Fires on the newline/space token immediately preceding a character name/speaker label that follows a closing line of dialogue, marking the transition point between one character's speech and the next speaker tag

40.label: speaker label after line break
40.description: Fires on the newline/space character immediately preceding a new speaker's name tag in Shakespeare dramatic dialogue

41.label: demonstrative 'these' as determiner
41.description: The feature fires on the word 'these' when used as a demonstrative determiner modifying a following noun phrase, referring back to previously mentioned entities or concepts

42.label: "of" or "true" after speech/thought verb
42.description: The feature fires on the words 'of' or 'true' appearing immediately after verbs or phrases of speaking, thinking, or asserting (e.g. 'speak of', 'think of', 'tis true', 'tell me true'), marking the completion of a propositional complement or affirmation in dialogue.

43.label: BRABANTIO speaker tag
43.description: The feature activates on the 'IO' token at the end of the speaker label 'BRABANTIO' in dramatic dialogue stage directions

44.label: ch digraph
44.description: The feature activates on the 'ch' digraph or its component letters, particularly the 'ch' sequence appearing within words (arch, wine→nine, church, rich, enrich, enfranchised, etc.), often at a morpheme boundary or within a syllable containing the 'ch' sound or adjacent vowel+consonant pattern

45.label: question mark closing interrogative
45.description: The feature fires on '?' characters that close direct questions in dialogue, particularly yes/no or rhetorical questions addressed to a specific person

46.label: mid-word or word-final position in polysyllabic words
46.description: Feature fires on tokens that are the latter part of a word split across tokens, specifically word-internal or word-final subword tokens like 'please', 'For', 'ving', 'ished', 'ished', 'ished', 'ow'

47.label: quoted or emphasized word follows imperative/directive
47.description: Feature activates on words that are direct objects or key nouns immediately following imperative verbs or directive phrases, often in command or confrontational contexts

48.label: or
48.description: The feature detects the letter sequence 'or' appearing within words, firing on tokens that contain or end with the substring 'or'

49.label: question mark followed by newline/speaker change
49.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or line break immediately following a question mark at the end of a spoken question, just before a new character's speech begins

50.label: un- prefix
50.description: The feature activates on the token 'un' as a prefix in words beginning with 'un-' (e.g., unperceived, unrespective, unsubstantial, unfeeling, unloose, unregarded, unfair, unwillingly, unless, unmatched, unawares, united)

51.label: speaker label after line break
51.description: The feature fires on whitespace/newline tokens immediately preceding a character name/speaker label in Shakespeare dramatic dialogue

52.label: FALSTAFF speaker tag
52.description: The feature activates on the character name 'FALSTAFF' when it appears as a speech attribution/speaker label in the dramatic text

53.label: speaker label after line break
53.description: Fires on the newline/space character immediately preceding a character name (speaker label) that begins a new speech turn in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue

54.label: put/puts
54.description: The feature detects the verb 'put' or 'puts' (in various syntactic contexts) from Shakespeare's works

55.label: sentence-ending punctuation after line climax
55.description: Feature fires on sentence-ending punctuation marks (!, ?, .) that conclude a complete clause or rhetorical statement, particularly at the end of a character's spoken line before a new speaker or continuation

56.label: end of speech/clause period
56.description: Feature activates on period punctuation marks that close a line of dialogue or clause in Shakespeare, typically mid-scene at a speaker turn boundary

57.label: Character name 'POMPEY' (or similar speaker label)
57.description: Fires on the token 'POMPEY' when it appears as a dramatic speaker/character label introducing dialogue, occasionally firing on similar speaker labels like 'ANGELO'

58.label: speaker tag after dialogue
58.description: Feature activates on the newline/space token immediately preceding an all-caps speaker label (stage direction attribution) that follows a line of dialogue in Shakespeare's plays

59.label: possessive 'lord/duke' reference
59.description: The feature activates on the word 'lord' (or occasionally 'duke') when used as a possessed or relational noun referring to a master, husband, or noble superior, typically preceded by a possessive pronoun or modifier (my lord, your lord, my lord's, etc.)

60.label: end of sentence newline
60.description: Activates on whitespace/newline tokens that immediately follow the end of a complete sentence (period or similar punctuation), marking sentence boundaries in dialogue or verse

61.label: in within words
61.description: The feature detects the letter sequence 'in' appearing inside words, particularly the substring 'in' within 'instinct', 'point', 'popinjay', 'Dauphin', etc.

62.label: end of stanza/couplet
62.description: Feature fires on the newline or blank character immediately following the closing guillemet of a rhyming couplet or stanza-ending line in verse, marking stanza boundaries in Shakespeare's poetry

63.label: short declarative sentence ending
63.description: Fires on punctuation (period or exclamation mark) that closes a short, complete utterance (typically a brief command, statement, or exclamation of a few words) in Shakespearean dramatic dialogue

64.label: first-person object pronoun
64.description: The feature activates on the word 'me' used as an object pronoun in first-person constructions across various syntactic roles (direct object, indirect object, object of preposition)

65.label: newline/speaker break
65.description: Detects the newline or whitespace token immediately following a closing punctuation mark that precedes a new speaker label or stage direction in dramatic dialogue

66.label: complementizer 'that'
66.description: The word 'that' functioning as a complementizer or relative pronoun introducing a subordinate clause

67.label: of (genitive/partitive)
67.description: The word 'of' appearing after a noun phrase, typically in a genitive, partitive, or descriptive construction (e.g., 'rarity of', 'soul of', 'hand of', 'powers of')

68.label: on syllable
68.description: The feature activates on the letters 'on' when they form the ending syllable of a proper name (e.g., Palamon, Cleon, Lyca'on'ia, Paphlag'on'ia, Dagonet), particularly names of characters or places in Shakespeare's plays

69.label: Character name tag 'W'
69.description: The feature fires on the letter 'W' (or occasionally a multi-character token) that appears as part of a character name speaker label in a dramatic script, most prominently in 'FRIAR LAWRENCE' (the 'W' in LAWRENCE) and 'COBWEB' (the 'W' in COBWEB), marking the position within an all-caps dramatic speaker designation.

70.label: «an» substring
70.description: The feature fires on the token 'an' as part of a larger word, specifically the substring 'an' appearing within words (e.g., 'appliances', 'humanity', 'Adriano', 'Regan', 'Tyrian', 'Montano', 'Volscian', 'grievances', 'humane', 'human', 'Adriana')

71.label: newline/whitespace after short utterance
71.description: Fires on whitespace or newline tokens that immediately follow a short spoken line or reply in dramatic dialogue, acting as a turn boundary marker between speaker exchanges

72.label: thou + function_word
72.description: Feature activates on function words (prepositions, conjunctions, conditionals) that immediately follow the word 'thou' or appear in close proximity to second-person singular 'thou' constructions

73.label: be/because token
73.description: Fires on the token 'be' whether standalone or as the start of a word like 'because', 'bells', 'beads'

74.label: question mark after short exclamation or address
74.description: Feature fires on '?' or '.' punctuation marks that immediately follow a short reactive exclamation, single word, or brief phrase (often a character's shocked/surprised response like 'No?', 'What?', 'How now?', 'An honor?') at the start of a new speaker's turn in dramatic dialogue

75.label: motion into enclosed space
75.description: The word 'into' following a verb of movement or placement, indicating physical or metaphorical entry into a container, location, or bounded space

76.label: infinitive/auxiliary + newline gap
76.description: Feature fires on a space or line-break token that immediately follows a verb (often 'have', 'let', 'make', 'pluck', etc.) where the syntax bridges across a line break, capturing the position between a verb and its following complement across a verse-line boundary

77.label: adverb/particle in mid-clause position
77.description: The feature fires on adverbs or particles (then, still, danger, ly, nce, ly, answer, yet, ly, then) appearing in the middle of a clause, typically between a modal/auxiliary verb and the main verb or complement

78.label: newline after question mark
78.description: Feature fires on whitespace/newline characters that immediately follow a question mark, typically at dialogue or stanza boundaries in dramatic verse

79.label: line-initial newline token
79.description: The feature fires on whitespace/newline tokens that appear at the start of a new line of verse or prose, immediately following a comma or end of a clause, marking a line break in Shakespeare's text

80.label: Capital B at line/clause start
80.description: The feature fires on the capital letter 'B' when it begins a new clause, sentence, or poetic line following punctuation or a line break

82.label: comma at line-end
82.description: A comma appearing at the end of a poetic line (before a line break), functioning as a mid-sentence pause or enjambment marker in verse

83.label: question mark closing short utterance
83.description: Feature activates on '?' punctuation that closes a brief spoken question or exclamation in dialogue, typically a short interrogative phrase of a few words directed at another character

84.label: French/Latin text endings
84.description: Detects word-final characters or subword tokens that are parts of French or Latin words, particularly common grammatical endings like 'x', 'es', 'ens', 'sub' occurring within non-English (French/Latin) passages in Shakespeare's Henry V

85.label: letter k sound
85.description: The feature activates on the letter 'k' or letters producing a /k/ sound (ck, k), particularly mid-word or at syllable boundaries

86.label: first-person present tense 'am'
86.description: The feature fires on the word 'am' when used as a first-person singular present tense copula (I am), capturing the syntactic pattern of the subject 'I' followed by the verb 'am'

87.label: indefinite article 'a'
87.description: The feature fires on the indefinite article 'a' (or the start of words beginning with 'a' like 'aged', 'aye', 'aught') appearing in poetic or prose contexts from Shakespeare's works

88.label: line break / stanza boundary
88.description: Activates on the whitespace or empty token immediately following a line-ending punctuation mark (period, semicolon) that represents a poetic line break in Shakespeare's verse, i.e. the newline/space token between successive verse lines

89.label: int/G proper noun fragment
89.description: Activates on the token 'int' (as part of 'intent') and the letter 'G' when it begins a proper name (person or place), capturing these specific subword token fragments in Shakespearean context

90.label: preposition 'on'
90.description: The feature fires on the word 'on' used as a preposition or particle in various contexts (look on, go on, stir on, made on, etc.)

91.label: of/to/with preposition
91.description: Feature fires on prepositions (primarily 'of', occasionally 'to'/'with'/'thy') that follow a noun in a genitive/partitive phrase, typically within poetic or dramatic verse constructions

92.label: didst/couldst/wouldst
92.description: Second-person singular past tense archaic verb forms ending in '-st' suffix, typically in questions

93.label: speech-initial function words
93.description: Tokens that appear at or near the start of a character's spoken line, typically interrogative, superlative, ordinal, or directional words (What, First, Most, Right) that open a new speech turn in dramatic dialogue

94.label: third-person singular past/present auxiliary
94.description: The feature fires on third-person singular forms of 'to be' or 'to have' (was, hath, has) used as auxiliary or copular verbs in subordinate or relative clauses referring to a third-person subject (he, she, it)

95.label: hath after subject
95.description: The feature fires on the word 'hath' when it immediately follows a noun phrase or pronoun subject, capturing the archaic third-person singular present tense verb 'hath' in subject-verb constructions.

96.label: modal verb
96.description: The feature activates on modal or auxiliary verbs (can, cannot, would, may, 'd) that introduce a subordinate or conditional clause, often in contexts of possibility, consequence, or conditional reasoning

97.label: sentence-final punctuation after short utterance
97.description: Fires on period or exclamation mark that closes a short, complete dramatic utterance or response (typically a brief line of dialogue ending a sentence)

98.label: line-initial position after clause boundary
98.description: The feature fires on whitespace or the first token of a new syntactic/verse line following a major clause or sentence boundary, often after punctuation like periods, commas, or semicolons that mark a transition to a new clause or line of verse

99.label: all/call/fall/swallow substring
99.description: Tokens containing the letter sequence 'all' (as a standalone word or embedded in words like 'call', 'fall', 'swallow', 'ballad')

100.label: give/gives conjugations
100.description: The token is a conjugated form of the verb 'give' (give or gives), appearing in various syntactic and thematic contexts throughout Shakespeare's works

101.label: '-ure' or '-em' suffix tokens
101.description: The feature activates on tokens that are suffixes or partial word endings, particularly '-ure', '-em', '-ure', and similar phonological/orthographic endings appearing mid-word after a split tokenization

102.label: ur digraph in word
102.description: The feature activates on the letter sequence 'ur' appearing within words, particularly in words like 'scurvy', 'turmoiled', 'church', 'curling', 'accursed', 'scurrilous'

103.label: good as greeting/farewell modifier
103.description: The feature activates on the word 'good' when it appears as part of a formulaic greeting or farewell phrase (e.g., 'good morrow', 'good night', 'good cheer') or as a positive evaluative adjective in socially polite or transitional discourse contexts in Shakespearean dialogue

104.label: beseech/address your [title]
104.description: Formal supplication or address phrase containing 'your' followed by an honorific title (Grace, Majesty, Highness, Honor, Lordship), typically in a deferential or petitionary context

105.label: infinitive 'tell'
105.description: The feature fires on the word 'tell' (or parts thereof) used as an infinitive or bare verb form, typically in constructions like 'to tell', 'will tell', 'I'll tell', or 'doth tell'

106.label: blank line / paragraph break
106.description: The feature fires on whitespace or newline tokens that represent a scene or speech break between lines of dialogue or verse in Shakespeare's text

107.label: article 'the' before newline/space+word
107.description: Fires on a space or newline character that immediately follows the definite article 'the', capturing the boundary between 'the' and the following noun phrase

108.label: stressed adjective suffixes
108.description: Feature fires on suffixes of adjectives with negative or heavy connotations, particularly '-less', '-ous', and 'heavy' used as modifiers, often in emotionally weighted contexts

109.label: 'you'/'I' after 'will'
109.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'you' or 'I' immediately following the modal verb 'will', typically in direct address or conditional constructions in Shakespearean dialogue

110.label: abstract noun mid-word or post-adjective
110.description: The feature activates on nouns denoting abstract or elevated concepts (beauty, addition, ambition, kingdom, bosom, father) that appear as the focal/head noun following a determiner, adjective, or preposition in a poetic or rhetorical context

111.label: QUEEN speaker label
111.description: The feature fires on the token 'QUEEN' when it appears as a character speech prefix/stage direction label in Shakespeare's plays, indicating a queen character is about to speak

112.label: 'The' article token
112.description: Fires on the word 'the' (capitalized or lowercase) when it appears mid-sentence following a clause boundary, comma, or line break, often introducing a noun phrase after a syntactic pause

113.label: Coriolanus political context
113.description: Tokens appearing in politically charged passages from Coriolanus, particularly relating to Roman governance, tribunes, consulship, and conflict between Martius/Coriolanus and the Roman state

114.label: Capital C after space/punctuation
114.description: The feature fires on the letter 'C' at the start of a proper noun (name or place), typically following a space or punctuation mark, in Roman/Shakespearean dramatic contexts

115.label: character name abbreviation
115.description: Firing on abbreviated or truncated character name tokens in stage directions or speech prefixes, where the speaker label is cut mid-word

116.label: How (interrogative/exclamatory opener)
116.description: The feature fires on the word 'How' when it appears at the start of a spoken line or question in Shakespearean dialogue, typically as an interrogative or exclamatory opener by a character

117.label: tre- prefix
117.description: Tokens beginning with the substring 'tre' (e.g., tremble, treachery, treasure, tread, trees, treacherous)

118.label: preposition 'in' mid-sentence
118.description: The feature detects the word 'in' (or the 'in' prefix in 'invented') appearing mid-sentence in dialogue, typically following a verb or auxiliary and preceding a noun phrase or location

119.label: Character name starting with C or N
119.description: The feature fires on the first letter of a character name (specifically 'C' for Cymbeline and 'N' for Nerissa/Nun/Nathaniel) appearing as a speech prefix/stage direction label in Shakespeare's dramatic text

120.label: sentence-ending punctuation after clause
120.description: Fires on punctuation marks (period, exclamation mark) that end a complete syntactic clause or sentence, particularly in dialogue

121.label: se/vi syllable onset
121.description: The feature fires on tokens beginning with the syllable 'se' or 'vi', capturing a specific phonological/orthographic pattern at the start of a token

122.label: comma after negation clause
122.description: Feature fires on commas that immediately follow clauses containing 'not' or other negation words, marking a syntactic boundary after a negated proposition

123.label: word starting with 'C' or 'c'
123.description: The feature fires on tokens that are the letter 'C' (uppercase or lowercase) at the start of a word, particularly content words beginning with 'c' such as 'care', 'Corn', 'Call', 'created', 'could', 'copper', 'cut', 'cry', 'considered', 'Could'

124.label: with (preposition)
124.description: The feature fires on the word 'with' used as a preposition in various syntactic contexts throughout Shakespeare's works

125.label: great/greatest
125.description: The feature activates on the word 'great' (or its superlative/comparative forms like 'greatest', 'greatness') across various syntactic and thematic contexts in Shakespeare

126.label: Capitalized S in proper nouns
126.description: The feature fires on uppercase letter 'S' at the start of proper nouns (names, titles, places) mid-sentence

127.label: cont- prefix token
127.description: The feature fires on the token 'cont' as part of words beginning with the prefix 'cont-' (contain, contrary, contend, continuously, contumelious, contrived, contents, contending), capturing this specific subword token at the start of 'cont-' prefixed words

128.label: Character name before speech
128.description: Fires on the whitespace or line break token immediately following a character's name (speaker label) in a Shakespeare play script, just before their dialogue begins

129.label: exam- morpheme
129.description: The feature activates on the token 'am' as part of the morpheme 'exam-' in words like 'examine', 'example', 'examination', 'exclamation', 'encamp'

130.label: motion/return verb
130.description: The feature activates on forms of 'return' (and occasionally similar motion/arrival verbs like 'come' and 'way') in contexts involving physical or metaphorical movement back to a place or person

131.label: sp token
131.description: The feature fires on the token 'sp' which is a fragment of words beginning with 'sp' (spite, spy, specialties, spend, spare, spread, spell, splitting, sparingly) representing the 'sp' consonant cluster at the start of a word or word segment

132.label: Thou + verb
132.description: Second-person singular archaic verb forms following 'Thou', including contracted forms like 'tell'st', 'took'st', 'bear'st', 'know'st', capturing the 'Thou [verb]' construction in Early Modern English

133.label: imperative 'take'
133.description: The feature fires on the word 'take' used as an imperative verb (command form), typically following words like 'therefore', 'then', or 'go' in direct commands or instructions.

134.label: KING speaker label
134.description: Fires on the token 'KING' when it appears as a dramatic character speaker label/stage direction in Shakespeare plays

135.label: adverb/particle in recovery or resumption context
135.description: Tokens like 'again', 'well', 'alive', 'heaven', 'ity' appearing after expressions of negation, doubt, harm, or interrupted action — words signaling restoration, repetition, or affirmation following adversity

136.label: he
136.description: The feature detects the word 'he' (or the start of words beginning with 'he' like 'hen', 'heigh') used as a pronoun or word-initial substring referring to a male character in dialogue

137.label: too (intensifier/adverb)
137.description: The feature fires on the word 'too' used as an intensifier or adverb meaning 'excessively' or 'also', typically in constructions like 'are too X' or ''tis too X'

138.label: noble succession/lineage
138.description: Tokens appearing in contexts about royal or noble succession, inheritance, and aristocratic lineage (dukes, heirs, succession claims)

139.label: word starting with 'f'
139.description: The feature fires on tokens that are the letter 'f' at the start of a word, particularly in mid-sentence positions across various Shakespeare plays

140.label: indefinite article 'a'
140.description: The feature fires on the indefinite article 'a' (or the start of a word beginning with 'a' following a space, functioning as the article)

141.label: semicolon or comma clause boundary
141.description: Feature fires on semicolons and occasional commas that mark major syntactic clause boundaries between independent or parallel clauses in verse and prose

142.label: sm/kn digraph
142.description: Tokens beginning with the two-letter sequences 'sm' or 'kn'

143.label: boy/boys or compound forms
143.description: The feature activates on the word 'boy', 'boys', or morphemes containing 'boy'/'beast' as part of compound or inflected forms (boyish, beastliness), detecting the lexical stem related to young males or animalistic inferiority

144.label: demonstrative 'This' starting new sentence
144.description: The word 'This' appearing at the beginning of a new sentence or clause, typically after a period or sentence boundary, used as a demonstrative pronoun or determiner to introduce a consequential statement

145.label: speaker label after line break
145.description: The feature fires on newline/whitespace tokens that immediately precede a character name/speaker label in Shakespeare dramatic dialogue, marking the transition point between one character's speech and the next speaker's cue

146.label: third-person singular present tense 's' suffix
146.description: The feature fires on the letter 's' that forms the third-person singular present tense verb ending (e.g., 'drinks', 'bids', 'weeps', 'scorns', 'longs', 'dwells'), distinguishing this inflectional morpheme from other uses of 's'

147.label: '-er' suffix
147.description: Tokens ending in the letters 'er', often appearing mid-word as part of suffixes or word-final 'er' sounds (e.g., 'er' in 'never', 'shoulder', 'offer', 'canker', 'over')

148.label: speaker label after line break
148.description: The feature fires on the whitespace/newline character immediately preceding a character's speech attribution (speaker name) in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue format

149.label: end of short declarative sentence
149.description: Period ending a short, complete declarative or imperative sentence (typically 5-10 words) spoken in dialogue, often followed by a new speaker or contrasting statement

150.label: FIRST character cue
150.description: The feature fires on the token 'FIRST' when it appears as part of a stage direction or speaker label (e.g., 'FIRST LORD', 'FIRST SOLDIER', 'FIRST GUARD'), identifying numbered/ordinal character designations in Shakespearean dramatic text.

151.label: OF in character name
151.description: The word 'OF' appearing as part of a character's name/label in Shakespeare's play (e.g., 'ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS', 'DROMIO OF SYRACUSE'), specifically the 'OF' token within these compound character identifiers

152.label: comma after affirmative/interjection
152.description: A comma immediately following a short affirmative or interjective word (Ay, Yes, Ha, etc.) or a title/salutation, typically at the start of a character's spoken response

153.label: second person verb phrase
153.description: Feature activates on function words (prepositions, auxiliaries, verbs) immediately following 'you' in direct address or second-person constructions

154.label: bid/bids
154.description: The feature detects occurrences of the word 'bid' or 'bids' (in various conjugations), specifically the verb meaning to command, invite, or urge someone to do something

155.label: preposition 'from'
155.description: The feature activates on the word 'from' used as a preposition indicating movement, origin, or separation

156.label: request or permission to speak
156.description: The word 'speak' appears in contexts where a character is asking for, being granted, or being denied the opportunity to speak (e.g., 'let me speak', 'I will not hear thee speak', 'leave to speak')

157.label: ri- word initial syllable
157.description: The feature activates on the letters 'ri' at the start of a word or syllable, capturing the orthographic/phonological sequence 'ri' in words like 'rid', 'ripe', 'riding', 'rival', 'riddle', 'rigorous', etc.

158.label: word-final substring
158.description: Feature activates on tokens that are the final characters/syllable of a word, typically appearing mid-word-completion or at word boundaries where the preceding context ends a morpheme (e.g., 'age', 'ory', 'ade', 'aking', or space+complete-word like ' better', ' done', ' me')

159.label: adverb 'so' as intensifier
159.description: The feature fires on the word 'so' used as a degree adverb/intensifier modifying adjectives, adverbs, or verbs in Shakespearean dialogue

160.label: ra- prefix fragment
160.description: The feature activates on the letters 'ra' at the start of a word root, capturing word fragments beginning with 'ra' such as 'raised', 'ravished', 'rail', 'ravel', 'racked', 'railed' — specifically the 'ra' bigram at a morpheme boundary following a word break

161.label: 'And/Yet/When' followed by comma
161.description: Feature activates on a comma that immediately follows a sentence-initial coordinating conjunction or adverb (And, Yet, When) used as a discourse connector at the start of a clause

162.label: phrasal verb 'out'
162.description: The feature fires on the word 'out' when it functions as a particle in a phrasal verb construction (e.g., 'outrun', 'push out', 'speak out', 'find out', 'fall out', 'keep out', 'flout out', 'look out'), typically following a verb and preceding a prepositional phrase or object.

163.label: newline after punctuation/speech
163.description: Fires on a newline or whitespace character that follows closing punctuation or a speech tag boundary, marking the transition point between one clause/line of verse and the next in dialogue

164.label: character name label
164.description: The feature fires on character names (speaker labels) in Shakespeare's dramatic texts, particularly 'SEBASTIAN', 'CORIOLANUS', and similar proper names appearing as dialogue attribution headers.

165.label: I [modal/auxiliary verb]
165.description: The feature fires on modal or auxiliary verbs (was, could, should, shall, may) immediately following the first-person pronoun 'I'

166.label: is/was verb form
166.description: The feature activates on the tokens 'is', 'was', and 'wash' (tokenized as 'was'+'h'), capturing the verb forms 'is' and 'was' (copula/auxiliary) as well as subword tokens beginning with 'was'

167.label: adv- prefix
167.description: The feature activates on the token '«adv»' as a morpheme/subword token representing the prefix 'adv-' at the start of words like 'advise', 'adventure', 'advantage', 'advocate', 'advance'

168.label: 'What [noun]' interrogative phrase
168.description: The feature activates on the noun immediately following the interrogative word 'What' in 'What [noun]' constructions, typically forming a question or exclamation in Shakespearean dialogue

169.label: w-word after token boundary
169.description: The feature fires on tokens beginning with 'w' (or occasionally 'qu') that follow an apostrophe or space, particularly in archaic/subjunctive constructions like 'wert', 'wage', 'waste', 'winged', 'widow', as well as 'quite', capturing words starting with 'w' in mid-sentence positions within Shakespearean prose

170.label: ear/love substring
170.description: Tokens that contain the substring 'ear' or 'love' (including within larger words like 'early', 'earnestly', 'lovely')

171.label: semicolon at end of clause
171.description: The feature fires on semicolons (;) that appear at the end of a poetic or rhetorical clause, typically marking a pause between parallel or contrasting statements in verse or prose

172.label: de- prefix or 'de' particle
172.description: The token 'de' appearing either as a French/Latin particle in proper names (e.g. 'de la', 'Coeur de Lion') or as the start of an English word with the 'de-' prefix (e.g. 'demanded', 'deemed', 'defeat', 'dews', 'demeanor', 'decked')

173.label: auxiliary 'have' in perfect constructions
173.description: The feature fires on the word 'have' when used as an auxiliary verb forming perfect aspect (present perfect or past perfect), typically mid-clause after a subject pronoun or noun phrase

174.label: '-al' or '-ign' suffix syllable
174.description: The feature fires on the final syllable of words ending in '-al' (trial, prodigal, vassal, requital, universal, burial) or occasionally '-ign' (designs), particularly the 'al'/'ign' token at word boundaries

175.label: st- word continuation
175.description: The feature fires on the letters 'st' that begin a word, specifically where 'st' appears as the start of a word token following a word boundary, capturing word-initial 'st' clusters across various words (stop, sterling, stubborn, stars, statue, staff, stuck, started, station, storm, stones, step)

176.label: sentence-ending punctuation
176.description: Period or exclamation mark ending a clause or sentence in Shakespearean dialogue

177.label: causal/explanatory 'For'
177.description: The feature fires on the conjunction 'For' used to introduce a causal, explanatory, or justifying clause (meaning 'because' or 'since'), typically at the start of a new clause following a statement

178.label: letter g in middle of word
178.description: The feature fires on the letter 'g' appearing within or at the boundary of words, particularly where 'g' follows another consonant or vowel in the middle or end of a word (gifts, length, engines, degrees, daggers, loggerhead, ugly, etc.)

179.label: villain or similar moral epithet
179.description: The feature activates on the word 'villain' (or morphologically similar words like 'villainy') and related strong moral condemnation terms like 'trespass', used as an accusatory epithet directed at a person

180.label: fa/live syllable onset
180.description: The feature activates on the letters 'fa' (as in 'fairs', 'fabric', 'favors', 'fares', 'faulty', 'factious', 'fails') and the word 'live', capturing a phonetic/orthographic pattern around the 'f+a' digraph onset and the word 'live'

181.label: sentence-initial 'There'
181.description: The feature fires on the word 'There' (or 'There's/There'll') when it appears at the start of a new sentence or independent clause, often following a period, speaker attribution, or punctuation boundary

182.label: Th- sentence start
182.description: The feature fires on 'Th' at the beginning of a new clause or sentence, specifically the digraph 'Th' starting words like 'Those', 'Three', 'Thriftless', 'Thrives' that appear at the start of a syntactic unit following a sentence boundary or major clause break

183.label: sentence-initial 'In'
183.description: The feature fires on the word 'In' when it appears at the start of a new sentence or clause, typically following punctuation or a speaker label

184.label: Speaker change newline
184.description: Whitespace or newline token immediately following a period or end of speech, preceding a new speaker label in Shakespeare dialogue

185.label: relative/conjunctive 'where(in)'
185.description: The feature fires on the word 'where' or 'wherein' functioning as a relative pronoun or subordinating conjunction introducing a relative or adverbial clause, typically following a noun or phrase that specifies a location, context, or domain

186.label: preposition 'on' after verb phrase
186.description: The feature fires on the word 'on' when it follows a verb or imperative, functioning as part of a phrasal verb or directional/object prepositional phrase in commands or action statements

187.label: sk- words
187.description: The feature activates on the token 'sk' at the beginning of words starting with 'sk' (sky, skill, skin, skull, skins)

188.label: pt cluster
188.description: Tokens containing the substring 'pt' (as in tempt, wept, slept, sumptuous, voluptuous, description)

189.label: imperative verb at clause start
189.description: The feature activates on imperative verbs (primarily 'Give', also 'make', 'take') that appear at the beginning of a new clause or sentence, functioning as direct commands

190.label: short negative/limiting adverb or quantifier
190.description: The feature activates on short words that express negation or limited quantity/degree, such as 'No', 'much', 'little', 'once', functioning as emphatic or contrastive discourse markers in dialogue

191.label: adversative conjunction 'But' at clause boundary
191.description: The feature fires on the word 'But' when it appears at the start of a new clause or sentence following a period or comma, functioning as an adversative/contrastive conjunction introducing a contrasting or qualifying statement in dialogue

192.label: can / canni-
192.description: The word 'can' (modal verb) or the morpheme 'can' at the start of 'cannibals', appearing as a standalone token or prefix

193.label: Capital V proper noun
193.description: The feature activates on the letter 'V' at the start of proper nouns, specifically names of places (Venice, Verona), peoples (Volsces/Volscians), or characters (Ventidius, Vincentio, Valentine) in Shakespeare's works

194.label: 'your' as honorific address
194.description: The feature fires on the word 'your' when used as part of a formal or honorific address (e.g., 'your Worship', 'your Majesty', 'your Lordship', 'your Graces') or in direct dialogue directed at a social superior or interlocutor in conversational/dramatic speech.

195.label: sentence-initial conjunction
195.description: Feature fires on 'And' or 'But' at the start of a new sentence or clause, typically following a period or major syntactic break

196.label: preposition 'at'/'this' in mid-clause
196.description: Feature fires on the preposition 'at' or demonstrative 'this' appearing mid-clause, typically following a verb phrase and preceding a noun phrase

197.label: medial syllable activation
197.description: The feature fires on a syllable or morpheme that occurs in the middle of a word, particularly in polysyllabic words where the token represents an internal chunk (e.g., 'ged' in 'tragedy', 'ful' in 'youthful', 'ract' in 'contracted', 'ried' in 'buried'), but also on short function words or names mid-phrase

198.label: Give me [object]
198.description: The feature fires on 'me' in the imperative phrase 'Give me [something]', a recurring Shakespearean command construction

199.label: comma after feminine pronoun referent
199.description: A comma appearing after a word that refers to or describes a female subject (she, her, beauty, etc.), typically mid-clause in poetic or dramatic verse

200.label: word starting with 'b'
200.description: The feature activates on the letter 'b' at the start of a word, specifically detecting word-initial 'b' characters in the text

201.label: comp- prefix
201.description: The feature activates on tokens spelling 'comp' as the beginning of words with the 'comp-' prefix (companion, compass, compact, complaint, competitor, compounds, etc.)

202.label: auxiliary 'will' future
202.description: The feature fires on the word 'will' when used as a future-tense or volitional auxiliary verb in Shakespearean prose or verse

203.label: With/Within at line start
203.description: The word 'With' or 'Within' (or 'Without') appearing at the beginning of a new clause or line, typically following a sentence break or major syntactic boundary

204.label: You at speech boundary
204.description: The feature fires on the word 'You' (or 'You'll', 'You're') when it appears at the start of a new speaker's turn or a new clause following a speaker tag, functioning as the sentence-initial second-person pronoun that opens a direct address or accusation.

205.label: court/cour- word family
205.description: Tokens that are part of the word 'court', 'courteous', 'courtesy', or 'courtesan' (the morpheme 'court')

206.label: "What is" inquiry prompt
206.description: The feature detects the word 'is' immediately following 'What' in a dramatic dialogue context, specifically the phrase 'What is' used as a character's interrogative response or question prompt in Shakespearean plays.

207.label: re- prefix split across tokens
207.description: The feature fires on 're' tokens that are the second part of a word beginning with 're-' (realm, revolt, reach, reeking, repeat, revolting), where the word has been tokenized by splitting after the 're' prefix

208.label: word starting with 'd'
208.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the letter 'd' beginning a word, particularly nouns and adjectives starting with 'd' (dagger, dancing, dame, darling, dower, daggers, dumb, dies, ducats, dowry, dignities, date)

209.label: title 'of' construction
209.description: The word 'of' appearing as part of a noble title or address, specifically in patterns like 'Lord of [Place]', 'Duke of [Place]', 'King of [Place]', 'Cousin of [Place]' referring to aristocratic titles in Shakespeare's historical plays

210.label: character_speech_tag
210.description: Feature activates on the newline/space token immediately following a character name label (e.g. 'URSULA', 'DUKE', 'LORD') in dramatic dialogue, marking the transition from speaker tag to spoken text

211.label: relative pronoun 'who' as clause introducer
211.description: The feature fires on the word 'who' when it introduces a relative or appositive clause that provides additional information about a previously mentioned person or noun phrase

212.label: 'I' after interjection/address
212.description: The first-person pronoun 'I' appearing immediately after a vocative address, interjection, or brief imperative phrase (e.g., 'Peace, I say', 'Nay, I', 'Well, sir, I'), marking a speaker's self-assertion following a call to attention

213.label: if I
213.description: The token 'I' immediately following the word 'if' (the conditional phrase 'if I')

214.label: speaker tag followed by dialogue
214.description: Feature fires on the space/newline immediately after a character name (speaker label) in dramatic dialogue, marking the transition point between speaker attribution and spoken text

215.label: letter k sound
215.description: The feature activates on the letter 'k' or letter sequences producing a /k/ sound, including silent 'k' in words like 'knave', 'knock', 'know', as well as 'k' in 'musk', 'thank', 'welkin', 'Turk', and 'unknown'

216.label: mis- prefix
216.description: Tokens that are the prefix 'mis' at the start of words with the mis- prefix (miscarry, mischief, misled, miscarried, misery, misfortunes, misbecome, mischance, misinterpret)

217.label: pronoun 'it' as object
217.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'it' when used as a direct object, typically following a verb (read it, break it, keep it, call it, forbid it, etc.)

218.label: i' th' / o' th' contraction
218.description: The feature detects the abbreviated/elided form 'th' as part of contracted preposition phrases like 'i' th'' (in the) or 'o' th'' (of the), a common Shakespearean poetic contraction for metrical purposes

219.label: line-break boundary
219.description: Fires on the space/newline token that immediately precedes a capitalized word beginning a new verse line or clause, marking a syntactic/metrical line boundary in Shakespeare's verse

220.label: over- prefix
220.description: Tokens that are the 'over' morpheme functioning as a prefix (or part of one) in compound words or phrasal verbs like 'overbear', 'overwhelmed', 'overhear', 'overreach', etc.

221.label: comma after noun/phrase mid-sentence
221.description: Fires on commas that follow a noun or short phrase in the middle of a poetic or prose clause, typically after a subject complement or object, creating a syntactic pause within verse lines

222.label: word fragment ending in token
222.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the final syllable or fragment of a longer word, where the token completes a word split across multiple tokens (e.g., 'ight' in 'flight'/'slight'/'weight', 'ter' in 'scepter', 'ers' in 'years', 'ledge' in 'knowledge', 'course', 'eight')

223.label: 'You' at start of new line/clause
223.description: The word 'You' appearing at the beginning of a new verse line or major clause boundary, typically after a line break or period in Shakespeare's text

224.label: character_name_followed_by_comma
224.description: Fires on commas that immediately follow a character name/speaker label, typically at the start of dialogue or in vocative address contexts

225.label: split prefix
225.description: The feature fires on the second character of a split two-character prefix or morpheme boundary (e.g., 'ag', 'f', 'th') where a word with a recognizable prefix (dis-, en-, un-, per-, etc.) is broken across a token boundary mid-prefix or mid-morpheme

226.label: Speaker label after speech
226.description: The feature activates on the name/title portion of a Shakespearean speaker label (e.g. 'KING RICHARD', 'SALISBURY', 'ALANSON') that appears after a preceding line of dialogue, specifically on the closing part of the speaker name token.

227.label: shall (future/obligation modal)
227.description: The feature fires specifically on the word 'shall' used as a modal verb expressing future intention, promise, or obligation in Shakespearean English

228.label: abstract noun phrase ending
228.description: The feature activates on the final syllable or word of an abstract or multisyllabic noun (often the last morpheme of a compound or derived noun like 'deliverance', 'lenity', 'deed', 'gold', 'death', 'hostility', 'shame', 'company', 'time', 'alone', 'days'), typically appearing at the end of a semantically weighty noun phrase within verse or prose

229.label: other
229.description: The feature fires on the word 'other' in various syntactic contexts (adjective, pronoun, part of 'each other')

230.label: emphatic repetition or echo token
230.description: Tokens that are part of repeated or echoed phrases, often emphatic restatements, corrections, or reinforcing expressions (e.g., 'Never, never', 'An old, an old', 'I tell thee...I tell thee', 'By gar...By gar')

231.label: Capitalized N/C proper nouns
231.description: The feature fires on capitalized letters that begin proper nouns or personifications, predominantly 'N' starting words like Neptune, Naples, Nature, Night, Nemesis, and occasionally other capitalized proper noun initials

232.label: first-person pronoun after address/salutation
232.description: The feature fires on 'I' when it appears immediately after a form of address, title, or name (e.g., 'Master X, I', 'Sir, I', 'Sir John, I'), marking the transition from vocative/salutation to the speaker's own statement

233.label: fore- prefix or 'fore' substring
233.description: The feature activates on tokens containing 'fore' as a prefix or substring, including words like 'forever', 'forest', 'forehead', 'forester', and 'forenoon', as well as the standalone token 'fore'

234.label: imperative_comma
234.description: The feature fires on commas that immediately follow an imperative verb (especially 'Go'), marking a command followed by a pause or address

235.label: preposition/adverb at line start
235.description: Feature fires on prepositions or adverbs (In, Under, For, half) that appear at or near the beginning of a new verse line in Shakespeare's iambic pentameter, often following a line break

236.label: set/put verb forms
236.description: The feature activates on conjugated forms of the verbs 'set' and 'put', particularly in past tense or third-person present constructions

237.label: quantitative determiners
237.description: The feature activates on number words or quantity words (like 'three', 'many', 'two') used as determiners or adjectives modifying a noun phrase, typically indicating a specific or approximate quantity

238.label: Thou + verb contraction
238.description: The feature fires on verb forms immediately following the archaic second-person pronoun 'Thou', particularly contracted or conjugated verbs in the pattern 'Thou [verb]'st' or 'Thou [verb]est'

239.label: sw/G digraph onset
239.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'sw' or capital 'G', likely detecting a specific two-character sequence at the start of a token

240.label: time period nouns
240.description: The feature activates on the words 'days' or 'years' when preceded by a specific number or quantity expression, indicating a measured span of time.

241.label: possessive or function word following proper noun/title
241.description: Feature fires on short function words or possessive markers ('s, new, unto, some, just) that appear immediately after a proper noun, title, or honorific (e.g. King Edward's, Cardinal's, Grace's, Robert's)

242.label: sentence-final period
242.description: The feature fires on period characters that end complete sentences or utterances, particularly in dialogue

243.label: Thy at sentence/clause start
243.description: The word 'Thy' appearing at the beginning of a new sentence or major clause boundary, typically after a period or as the first word of a direct address or speech

244.label: archaic second-person verb ending
244.description: The feature fires on the archaic English second-person singular verb inflection "'st" (as in "lov'st", "dar'st", "cop'st"), the contracted form of the "-est" ending used with "thou" in Early Modern English

245.label: Negative response word 'No'
245.description: The feature fires on the word 'No' when used as a direct spoken negation or contradiction in dialogue, typically appearing at the start of a character's reply

246.label: abstract noun after verb
246.description: Content words (nouns/adverbs like 'reason', 'cause', 'way', 'hope', 'enough') that follow a verb or determiner in contexts expressing manner, justification, or sufficiency

247.label: word starting with 'c'
247.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the letter 'c' beginning a word, particularly words starting with 'c' such as 'curious', 'cure', 'celestial', 'cunning', 'catching', etc.

248.label: sentence-final period
248.description: The feature activates on period punctuation marks that end sentences or complete clauses, particularly at clause boundaries in prose dialogue

249.label: relativized subject pronoun
249.description: The feature fires on 'we' or 'they' appearing immediately after a 'that' relativizer or complementizer, functioning as the subject of a subordinate clause introduced by 'that'

250.label: superlative 'most' or character name 'ALANSON'
250.description: The feature fires on the token 'most' when used as a superlative intensifier in direct address or praise, and also on the abbreviated character name 'ALANSON' as a speech prefix

251.label: copula linking verb
251.description: The feature fires on forms of 'to be' (is/was/were/been) functioning as a linking/copular verb connecting a subject to its complement

252.label: speaker tag transition
252.description: Activates on the newline or whitespace character immediately following a closing punctuation or speech segment, just before a new all-caps speaker name/stage direction begins

253.label: un- prefix
253.description: The feature fires on the token 'un' as the beginning of a word with the 'un-' negating prefix (e.g., unreversed, unrespective, unmatched, unbruised, unknown, unfit, unfolding, unkindness, unicorns, unseen, unfair)

254.label: indefinite article 'a'
254.description: The feature fires on the token 'a' functioning as an indefinite article, appearing before nouns or noun phrases in Shakespearean dialogue contexts

255.label: third-person singular present/past verb form
255.description: Detects conjugated forms of 'to be' or 'to do' in third-person singular (is, doth, did) following a noun or pronoun subject in declarative clauses

256.label: ant substring
256.description: The feature detects the substring 'ant' within words, particularly in positions like 'advant-', 'Plant-', 'cant', 'quant', 'gallant', 'corant', 'scant', 'fant'

257.label: medial consonant cluster
257.description: Tokens that are the middle portion of a word split across tokens, specifically containing consonant clusters or digraphs in the interior of multi-syllable words (e.g., 'ons', 'les', 'ci', 'qu', 'do', 'mo', 'ert')

258.label: man* token
258.description: The feature fires on the token 'man' (and the start of words containing 'man' such as 'manor', 'manner', 'mansion', 'man's'), detecting the lexical unit 'man' whether as a standalone word or as the beginning of a compound/derived word.

259.label: third-person masculine possessive 'his'
259.description: The feature activates on the word 'his' used as a third-person masculine possessive pronoun, typically referring to a male character previously mentioned in the surrounding text

260.label: negation with 'not'
260.description: The feature activates on the word 'not' in contexts expressing prohibition, refusal, or negation, often following modal verbs like 'must', 'shall', or 'do'

261.label: s-initial word after space
261.description: The feature fires on the letter 's' that begins a word following a space or word boundary, capturing word-initial 's' sounds across various grammatical contexts

262.label: word-internal 'be' prefix
262.description: Fires on the token 'be' when it is the beginning of a longer word (e.g., 'behavior', 'behalf', 'belt', 'beloved', 'bellows', 'behoof', 'beck', 'bearer'), detecting the morpheme 'be-' as a prefix inside a mid-word token split

263.label: definite article 'the' in poetic/literary context
263.description: Fires on the word 'the' appearing in Shakespeare's verse, particularly in poetic or figurative constructions involving sensory imagery, emotions, or metaphorical language

264.label: sentence-ending punctuation after short clause
264.description: Feature activates on period, comma, or question mark that terminates a short declarative or exclamatory clause, often following a proper noun or brief predicate

265.label: acc- prefix
265.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'acc' (the 'acc' fragment of words like 'accounted', 'accident', 'accounts', 'accomplish', 'accused', 'accuse', 'accusation', 'accord', 'accent')

266.label: ay-ending imperative verbs
266.description: Tokens that are the 'ay' suffix portion of imperative verbs like 'Play', 'Lay', 'Stay', 'Pay', where the word is used as a command or directive

267.label: word starting with 'm'
267.description: The feature activates on the letter 'm' at the start of a word, specifically when that 'm' follows a space (i.e., the initial 'm' character of words beginning with 'm' such as 'mutual', 'masons', 'milch', 'mood', 'miles', 'majesty', 'maintained', 'muttons', 'machine', 'match', 'mutiny')

268.label: spo- prefix fragment
268.description: The feature detects the token fragment 'spo' which begins words like 'spoils', 'spoken', 'spoons', representing a mid-word subtoken starting with the letters 'spo'

269.label: Speaker label after dialogue
269.description: Character name tag (often 'SENATOR', 'PERICLES', or 'CONSPIRATOR') appearing as a speaker attribution following a previous character's speech, typically at the start of a new speaking turn in dramatic text

270.label: some
270.description: The feature fires on the word 'some' used as a determiner or pronoun in various syntactic contexts across Shakespeare's works

271.label: end of short utterance
271.description: Period ending a brief speech or clause, typically at the conclusion of a short sentence or command within dialogue

272.label: pre- prefix
272.description: The feature activates on the token 'pre' as the beginning of words with the prefix 'pre-' (precious, prey, preposterous, predecease, prenzy, prepare, prefer, prevailing, preface)

273.label: ic/ion syllable ending
273.description: Tokens that are mid-word fragments ending in or constituting '-ic', '-ion', or similar Latinate suffix syllables, typically the final or penultimate syllable of a polysyllabic word

274.label: f-initial consonant cluster
274.description: The feature fires on the letter 'f' at the start of words beginning with 'fr-', 'fl-', or 'f' followed by a vowel or consonant, particularly in words like 'fruits', 'fleeced', 'fury', 'frolic', 'fought', 'fruitful', 'felt', 'fishes', 'frankly', 'foully', 'fought' — consistently the initial 'f' character of a content word

275.label: coordinating conjunction 'And' mid-sentence
275.description: Feature fires on 'And' used as a coordinating conjunction continuing or extending a clause within a longer compound or complex sentence, typically following a comma or end of a prior clause

276.label: Uppercase P
276.description: The feature activates on the uppercase letter 'P' at the start of words or names, particularly at the beginning of speech, proper nouns, or sentences.

277.label: newline/line-break token
277.description: Fires on whitespace or newline tokens that separate lines of verse or stage directions in poetic/dramatic text

278.label: comma after short utterance
278.description: A comma appearing immediately after a brief response or address (often 1-3 words) spoken by a character, typically at the start of a short reply or acknowledgment such as 'Nothing,' 'Welcome,' 'Not so,' etc.

279.label: look/sent verbs
279.description: The feature activates on the words 'look' or 'sent' in contexts where a character directly addresses another, often in second-person constructions ('you look', 'you sent')

280.label: Which/That I
280.description: Feature fires on 'I' when immediately preceded by 'Which' or 'That', forming a relative clause pattern 'Which I' or 'That I'

281.label: imp- prefix
281.description: Tokens beginning with the substring 'imp' (the prefix 'imp' as part of words like imprisonment, impurity, imposition, imply, imputation, impatient, important, impeached, impieties)

282.label: de- prefix
282.description: Tokens that are the 'de' prefix or start of words beginning with 'de' (demand, deceive, dearer, deck, deem, etc.)

283.label: We/I must
283.description: The feature fires on the word 'must' when immediately preceded by a first-person pronoun ('We' or 'I'), capturing the collocation of a subject pronoun + modal verb expressing necessity or obligation.

284.label: line break between rhyming couplet lines
284.description: Fires on the newline/whitespace token that separates the two lines of a rhyming couplet in Shakespearean verse, appearing between the end of one rhyming line and the start of the next

285.label: my lord address
285.description: The token 'my' in the phrase 'my lord' (or 'my good lord'), used as a respectful form of address in dialogue, typically appearing after a short interjection or sentence-initial position in a character's speech

286.label: lowercase letter v
286.description: The feature fires on the letter 'v' (lowercase), appearing mid-token or at the start of words, detecting the specific character 'v' in context

287.label: polite request phrase
287.description: The feature fires on 'pray' or 'beseech' in first-person polite request constructions (e.g., 'I pray you', 'I beseech you')

288.label: God token
288.description: The feature fires specifically on the token 'God' (or 'Gods'), detecting the word referring to a deity, whether used in oaths, prayers, invocations, or religious references.

289.label: preposition following object pronoun/noun
289.description: Feature fires on prepositions (primarily 'from', also 'off', 'unto') that immediately follow a noun or object pronoun (him, her, himself, etc.), indicating separation or movement away from something

290.label: em-dash interruption
290.description: The feature fires on em-dashes ('--') that mark a mid-sentence interruption, self-correction, or abrupt break in speech within Shakespeare's dialogue

291.label: here/there deictic
291.description: The feature activates on the words 'here' or 'there' used as locative/deictic adverbs, often at the start of a clause or introducing a new referent or location in dialogue

292.label: or
292.description: The word 'or' used as a coordinating disjunction between two alternatives, phrases, or clauses

293.label: possessive pronoun 'her'
293.description: The feature activates on the possessive pronoun 'her' used as a determiner/possessive adjective modifying a following noun, indicating ownership or association with a female referent

294.label: Character name tag
294.description: The feature fires on speaker label tokens (character names like QUEEN, JAQUES, JAILER) that appear as dialogue attributions in Shakespeare's dramatic texts

295.label: conjunction or function word mid-clause
295.description: Feature activates on common conjunctions and function words (and, when, does, else, if, that, state, if, all, with, he, and) that appear mid-sentence, typically following a comma or clause boundary, linking clauses or introducing subordinate structures

296.label: er substring
296.description: The feature fires on the letters 'er' appearing within a word, particularly in the middle or end of words containing this character sequence

297.label: Speaker transition marker
297.description: Detects the newline or whitespace token immediately following a closing speech segment, marking the transition to a new named speaker's dialogue turn in Shakespeare's dramatic text

298.label: har/i syllable onset
298.description: Tokens that begin with 'har' or 'i' appearing in Early Modern English contractions or words, specifically the subword tokens «har» and «i» as they appear mid-word in Shakespeare (e.g., 'harlot', 'harbor', 'harsh', and the contraction 'i'' as in 'i' th'')

299.label: gerund/present participle '-ing' suffix
299.description: The feature fires on the 'ing' token at the end of gerunds and present participles, particularly where the word is split so that 'ing' forms its own token

300.label: function word after feminine pronoun/noun context
300.description: Fires on small function words (conjunctions, auxiliary verbs, possessives, prepositions) that immediately follow or connect clauses centered on feminine subjects or feminine emotional/natural imagery

301.label: sa- prefix token
301.description: The feature fires on the token fragment «sa» which begins words like 'sacred', 'sail', 'sale', 'sacrifice', 'sable', 'sap', appearing as the first subtoken of these words after tokenization

302.label: letter 'e' in middle of word
302.description: The feature fires on the letter 'e' appearing within a word (not at word boundaries), particularly in common words like 'text', 'week', 'scene', 'bore', 'note', 'sue', etc.

303.label: learn/give stem
303.description: Tokens that are the beginning of words derived from 'learn' (learning, learned, learn) or forms of 'give' (give, given), particularly the root morphemes «lear» and «give»

304.label: pronoun after 'as'/'where'/'while'/'whereon'
304.description: Feature activates on personal pronouns (they/I) that immediately follow comparative or relative function words like 'as', 'where', 'while', 'whereon', typically in subordinate clauses

305.label: function words mid-clause
305.description: Common function words or short grammatical tokens (conjunctions, auxiliaries, adverbs, punctuation) appearing in the middle of a clause or sentence

306.label: character name ending in Y
306.description: Activates on the final letter 'Y' of character names/speaker labels (e.g. BERKELEY, ELY, STANLEY, WILLOUGHBY, WOLSEY, CATESBY) that end in the letter Y

307.label: imperative 'Go'
307.description: The feature fires on the word 'Go' (or occasionally 'Nay') used as a direct imperative command or dismissive exclamation at the start of a speech or clause in Shakespearean dialogue

308.label: monosyllabic common words
308.description: The feature activates on short, common monosyllabic words (read, sea, heart, die, face, together, over) that appear mid-sentence, often following auxiliary verbs or prepositions

309.label: Upon [possessive/determiner]
309.description: The feature fires on the word 'Upon' when it begins a phrase involving a possessive pronoun or determiner (e.g., 'Upon my', 'Upon thy', 'Upon his', 'Upon these'), typically at the start of a clause or oath-like declaration

310.label: conjunctive 'as'
310.description: The word 'as' used as a conjunction or adverbial connector introducing comparisons, manner clauses, or explanatory/temporal clauses

311.label: relative pronoun 'which'
311.description: The feature fires on the relative pronoun 'which' when used to introduce a relative clause, typically following a comma or punctuation

312.label: body part or abstract noun after indefinite article 'a'
312.description: Fires on nouns (especially body parts like 'hand' or abstract nouns like 'soul') that immediately follow the indefinite article 'a'

313.label: possessive 'my' after address/salutation
313.description: The feature fires on the word 'my' when it immediately follows a direct address, greeting, or salutation term (e.g., 'Welcome, my', 'Farewell, my', 'Ah, my', 'Here, my', 'Stay, my', 'Yes, my'), typically at the start of a character's speech turn in dramatic dialogue

314.label: future 'shall'
314.description: The feature activates on the word 'shall' used as a future/predictive modal auxiliary verb indicating future events or outcomes

315.label: Capital H or A after guillemets
315.description: Feature fires on tokens that are uppercase letters 'H' or 'A' appearing at the start of a proper noun or word, typically following a space or mid-word boundary

316.label: Capitalized P after 'the'
316.description: The feature fires on the letter 'P' at the start of a proper noun (name, title, or place) that is preceded by the word 'the', capturing this specific 'the + capitalized-P-word' syntactic/lexical pattern

317.label: Sir/Sirrah address term
317.description: The feature activates on the honorific or address terms 'Sir' and 'Sirrah' when used as direct forms of address to another character, often at the start of a speech or command

318.label: all
318.description: The word 'all' used in a collective or universal quantifier sense, typically following a verb or preposition in Shakespeare's works

319.label: my X and my Y
319.description: Conjunction 'and' linking two possessed items or concepts both preceded by first-person possessive 'my', forming a parallel 'my ... and my ...' or 'my ... and ...' structure

320.label: reflexive/abstract noun after preposition
320.description: Feature fires on abstract nouns or reflexive pronouns ('itself', 'fury', 'soul', 'grace', 'sense', 'state', 'cause', 'now') that immediately follow a preposition ('to', 'of', 'in') in a syntactic prepositional phrase

321.label: Character speech tag
321.description: Character name in all caps marking the beginning of a new speech turn in a Shakespeare play (stage dialogue attribution)

322.label: sentence-ending period
322.description: The feature fires on period characters that end sentences or independent clauses, particularly mid-paragraph sentence boundaries in prose or verse

323.label: abstract noun after verb/preposition
323.description: The feature fires on abstract nouns (love, heaven, help, care, warrant) appearing in emotionally or morally charged contexts, often as objects of verbs or prepositions expressing devotion, fate, or divine appeal

324.label: tri- prefix
324.description: The feature activates on tokens beginning with 'tri' (trifle, trick, triumph, trial), detecting this specific syllable/prefix sequence at the start of a word

325.label: perforce/scarce/cure suffix
325.description: Fires on 'ce' or 're' completing adverbs/words like 'perforce', 'scarce', or 'cure' — specifically the final syllable of these modal/adverbial terms indicating necessity or limitation

326.label: proper noun starting with 'A' after speaker/dialogue break
326.description: The feature fires on the letter 'A' that begins a proper noun (name or place) appearing at the start of a speech, exclamation, or new clause following a dialogue cue or punctuation break

327.label: short imperative/exclamatory phrase ending punctuation
327.description: The feature fires on exclamation marks or periods that terminate very short imperative commands or exclamatory interjections (e.g. 'Go to!', 'Away!', 'Peace!', 'Hark!'), typically 1-3 words spoken as sharp dramatic outbursts in dialogue

328.label: Capitalized C after space
328.description: The feature fires on the letter 'C' at the start of a proper noun or significant word following a space, capturing the initial capital letter of names and places (Chertsey, Crosby, Charmian, Cleon, Coventry, Caliban, Charles, Commodity, Count, Cupid, Cade, Cydnus)

329.label: adjective before noun
329.description: Positive or evaluative adjectives (sweet, true, wild, high, strange, new) appearing immediately before a noun or noun phrase, often in poetic or heightened literary contexts

330.label: 'Thou' at turn start
330.description: The feature fires on the word 'Thou' when it appears at the beginning of a spoken dialogue turn or direct address response, typically as the first word after a speaker attribution or conversational exchange in Shakespearean dialogue.

331.label: letter 'm' mid-token
331.description: Fires on the letter 'm' appearing inside a word (not at word start), particularly in sequences like '-rm', '-sm', '-mb', '-mp', '-mm' and similar mid-word 'm' positions

332.label: le- prefix
332.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'le' (legs, legacy, legions, level, levity, lewd, lean, leisure)

333.label: word starting with 'v'
333.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the letter 'v' beginning a word, capturing words like 'vows', 'vow', 'vanished', 'vengeance', 'vaulted', 'virginalling', 'vassal', 'vitlars', 'vouched'

334.label: such
334.description: The word 'such' used as a determiner or pronoun in context

335.label: sentence-ending period
335.description: Period (full stop) that ends a complete sentence or clause, typically mid-paragraph where dialogue or narrative continues after

336.label: speaker_turn_boundary
336.description: Activates on the newline or whitespace token immediately following a closing speech/dialogue line, marking the boundary between one character's dialogue ending and the next speaker tag beginning in Shakespeare's dramatic text

337.label: comparative 'as'
337.description: The word 'as' used in comparative or simile constructions (e.g., 'as X as Y', 'as if', 'as big as', 'as clear as')

338.label: Capital G word
338.description: The feature fires on the capital letter 'G' at the start of a word, typically at the beginning of a sentence or speech, including character names (GAUNT), common nouns (Gold, Guard), and adjectives (Great, Get, Gentlemen, Gardon)

339.label: By [oath/intensifier]
339.description: The word 'By' appearing at the start of an oath, exclamation, or emphatic expression (e.g., 'By my troth', 'By the Mass', 'By my sword', 'By the gods')

340.label: possessive 's after royal/familial noun
340.description: The feature fires on the possessive apostrophe-s ('s) token immediately following nouns denoting royalty, nobility, or close family relations (king, queen, father, brother, mother, grandsire, Edward, etc.)

341.label: line break after formal address
