341.description: The feature fires at the boundary between a formal honorific/title phrase and the start of a new clause or line, often at a newline or paragraph break following courtly address

342.label: light/black/dark contrast words
342.description: Words related to light, darkness, or black/white contrast, often in poetic or dramatic contexts involving illumination, vision, or moral contrast

343.label: al- prefix
343.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'al' (as part of words like 'aloud', 'altered', 'alike', 'alteration', 'alms', 'almanacs', 'alarm', 'alacrity', 'alchemy', 'alarums')

344.label: Exclamatory O vocative
344.description: The feature fires on the interjection 'O' used as an exclamatory vocative at the start of a speech, immediately followed by an address to a person, deity, or entity

345.label: letter 'c' in word
345.description: The feature fires on the letter 'c' within words, particularly where 'c' appears after the first character or mid-word, across various lexical and syntactic contexts

346.label: Letter J
346.description: The feature activates on the letter 'J' (capital J), appearing at the start of names (John, James, Japheth, Jesu, Jack, Jealous) or standalone J characters in Shakespeare's text

347.label: letter o
347.description: The feature fires on the letter 'o', particularly at the start of words or contractions like 'o'' (of), 'owes', 'owe', 'obedience', 'oozes', 'o'erteemed', appearing as a standalone letter or beginning of a word

348.label: sir response
348.description: The word 'sir' appearing as a direct address in short reply utterances, typically as a character's brief spoken response to being called or questioned

349.label: imperative exclamation
349.description: Short imperative commands or exclamations demanding silence, departure, or urgent action (e.g. 'Peace!', 'Away!', 'Be gone!')

350.label: infinitive/complement marker
350.description: Prepositions 'To' or 'Of' that introduce complement clauses or infinitive phrases following complex predicates or noun phrases, linking an action/state to its object or purpose

351.label: Capital T after speaker tag
351.description: The feature fires on a capital 'T' that begins a line of dialogue immediately following a speaker attribution tag (e.g., CHARACTER «T»rue, «T»ruly, «T»each, «T»om's, «T»onight), marking the start of a new speech turn in dramatic text.

352.label: all (universal quantifier)
352.description: The word 'all' used as a universal quantifier or intensifier in various syntactic positions (subject, object, adverbial)

353.label: Capital G word
353.description: The feature fires on the uppercase letter 'G' at the start of a word, occurring either as a character name/speaker label (GAUNT, GARDINER, GENTLEWOMAN) or at the beginning of a word following a line break or sentence boundary (Great, Grim-visaged, Gentlemen, Gold, Glory, Gaunt, Gertrude, Gorging)

354.label: coordinating conjunction 'and'
354.description: The feature fires on the word 'and' used as a coordinating conjunction connecting two nouns, adjectives, or noun phrases in a list or paired construction

355.label: eyes
355.description: The word 'eyes' as a noun referring to human eyes, typically in poetic or dramatic contexts

356.label: infinitive 'to'
356.description: The feature fires on the word 'to' when it introduces an infinitive verb phrase, capturing the syntactic role of 'to' as an infinitive marker

357.label: relative pronoun continuation
357.description: Fires on 'Which' or 'And' when it begins a relative or subordinate clause that continues or elaborates on the preceding noun phrase or clause

358.label: dev- stem or large number
358.description: Tokens that are either the stem 'dev' (as in 'devise/device') or the word 'thousand', capturing a lexical cluster around planning/scheming vocabulary and large quantities

359.label: letter t after apostrophe or word boundary
359.description: The feature fires on the letter 't' at the start of words or after apostrophes, particularly in words like 'tangled', 'table', 'to', 'tasking', 'tied', 'tailors', 'trespass', 'teeth', 'trenches', 'testiness', 'trodden', 'table' — specifically the initial 't' character token

360.label: possessive or contraction apostrophe-s
360.description: The feature fires on 's (possessive/contraction) and 'is' as a linking verb following a noun phrase, primarily detecting the apostrophe-s morpheme or its phonetic equivalent in Early Modern English text

361.label: letter 'a' in specific syllable position
361.description: The feature fires on the letter 'a' appearing as a stressed or prominent vowel within words, particularly in syllables like 'vault', 'vanguard', 'herald', 'hereafter', 'squares', 'vouchsafe', often mid-word in a stressed syllable

362.label: contrastive 'but'
362.description: The feature fires on the word 'but' when used as a contrastive or adversative conjunction connecting two contrasting clauses or ideas

363.label: polite address term
363.description: The feature activates on 'gentle' or 'good' used as honorific adjectives in direct address or salutation contexts, typically preceding a name or title (e.g., 'gentle mistress', 'gentle nurse', 'Good Signior')

364.label: I do not
364.description: The feature detects the word 'not' when it appears as part of the phrase 'I do not' (first-person negation construction) in Shakespearean dialogue

365.label: speaker label after line break
365.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or newline character immediately preceding a character's speech tag/name in dramatic dialogue, marking the transition point between one speaker's line and the next speaker's label

366.label: letters inside -ot/-okes/-ow/-atch/-kes word-internal sequences
366.description: The feature fires on consonant clusters or vowel sequences embedded within words, specifically on letters that are part of rhyme-like phonetic patterns such as 'ot', 'okes', 'ow', occurring mid-token in words like 'boot', 'plot', 'blot', 'spokes', 'strokes', 'shallow', 'watch', 'mandrakes', 'clothes'

367.label: cha- prefix
367.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'cha' (as in champion, chase, character, chaste, chaps)

368.label: Sir/Sirrah address
368.description: The feature fires on 'Sir' or 'Sirrah' used as a direct address or honorific at the start of speech or a clause, typically when a character is being addressed or spoken to formally/imperiously

369.label: line break whitespace
369.description: Whitespace tokens representing line breaks or stanza breaks in verse/poetry formatting

370.label: sentence-initial 'The'
370.description: The feature detects the word 'The' (or 'Their') appearing at the start of a new sentence or clause, typically after a period or other sentence boundary

371.label: feminine pronoun 'she' after clause boundary
371.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'she' when it appears as the subject of a new clause or phrase, often following a comma, conjunction, or other syntactic boundary, referring back to a previously mentioned female character

372.label: st/consonant_cluster_onset
372.description: Tokens that begin with or contain the letters 'st' (or similar consonant clusters like 'forb', 'ard') at a morpheme or syllable boundary, often mid-word splits in the tokenizer

373.label: space after em-dash or punctuation break
373.description: A space or newline token immediately following a double-dash (--) or similar punctuation break that marks a shift in address or aside in dialogue

374.label: word starting with 'm'
374.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the letter 'm' at the start of a word, particularly content words beginning with 'm' in poetic or prose contexts

375.label: subordinating conjunction 'that'
375.description: The feature fires on the word 'that' when used as a subordinating conjunction introducing a dependent clause, typically following verbs, adjectives, or nouns that set up a complement or relative clause

376.label: subordinating/coordinating conjunction at clause boundary
376.description: The feature fires on conjunctions and subordinators (Since, Though, And, For, That, If, From, Were) that introduce a new clause or subordinate clause, typically following a completed syntactic unit

377.label: first-person plural object
377.description: The feature fires on the word 'us' used as an object pronoun (direct, indirect, or prepositional object) in dialogue or speech contexts

378.label: re- prefix
378.description: The feature activates on the token 're' when it is the beginning of a word with the 're-' prefix (rend, redeemed, reign, received, revolt, reek, reveal, reformed, render, etc.), capturing the morphological pattern of re- prefixed words split across tokens.

379.label: "And" at dialogue boundary
379.description: The word 'And' appearing at the start of a new speech or clause boundary in Shakespearean dialogue, often following a period or speaker attribution, serving as a conjunctive opener to a new utterance or response

380.label: are
380.description: The feature detects the character sequence 'are', occurring either as the standalone word 'are', as part of the name 'Margaret', or embedded within other words like 'bleared', 'fare', 'spare'.

382.label: character name suffix -ON
382.description: The feature activates on the 'ON' token at the end of character names ending in '-ON' (e.g., CHATILLON, BRANDON, APPARITION) when they appear as dramatic speaker labels in Shakespeare's plays

383.label: phrasal verb particle
383.description: The feature fires on directional/aspectual particles (up, down, bed) that complete phrasal verbs or verb phrases, typically indicating completion, direction, or aspect of an action

384.label: speaker transition newline
384.description: Whitespace or newline character between a speech ending and a new speaker label in dramatic dialogue

385.label: comma after vocative/interjection
385.description: A comma immediately following a direct address, exclamation, or interjection (e.g., 'Peace,', 'Hail,', 'Rise,', 'Help,', 'Fie,', 'Boy,')

386.label: short common function words after punctuation or mid-clause
386.description: The feature fires on very common short function words (I, is, a) that appear in the middle of clauses, often following a period, comma, or quotation mark boundary

387.label: No, [continuation]
387.description: The token is a comma immediately following the word 'No' at the start of a character's spoken denial or refusal, i.e. the pattern 'No«,»' beginning a line of dialogue

388.label: speaker label after newline
388.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or newline character immediately following a character name/speaker label (e.g., PRINCE, TIMON, SHEPHERD) in dramatic dialogue format, specifically the space between the speaker tag and their spoken line

389.label: con- prefix token
389.description: Fires on the token fragment 'con' as the beginning of a word starting with the prefix 'con-' (conjured, converses, conjures, conceited, concerns, conceive, convey, conceit, conceal, conclude, converse, convenience)

390.label: Capitalized P mid-sentence
390.description: The feature fires on an uppercase letter 'P' that begins a word in the middle of a line (after a line break in verse), representing the start of a new verse line beginning with P following enjambment or mid-speech continuation

391.label: it/et substring
391.description: Tokens containing the substring 'it' or 'et' (case-insensitive), particularly mid-word occurrences of these two-letter sequences

392.label: Speaker label after newline
392.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or empty token immediately following a dramatic character name/speaker label (e.g. 'DUKE', 'FIRST LORD', 'SECOND LORD') in Shakespeare's play format, just before that character's dialogue begins

393.label: word starting with 'n'
393.description: The feature activates on words or word fragments beginning with the letter 'n' (nation, nurseth, nails, nigh, niece, number, nicely, niggard, nourished, nineteen, numbers, naked)

394.label: newline after 'thou' clause
394.description: Fires on whitespace/newline tokens that immediately follow a clause ending with 'thou' or a 'thou'-related verb phrase, marking a line break after second-person archaic address

395.label: le syllable
395.description: Token is the letters 'le' completing a word ending in the '-le' syllable (e.g. Orleans, castle, cripple, mantle, riddle, nuzzle, cradle, turtle, maypole, Carlisle)

396.label: shall/will/must be
396.description: The feature activates on the word 'be' when preceded by a modal verb (shall, will, must, may) forming a modal passive or future construction

397.label: newline/line-break token
397.description: The feature fires on whitespace or newline tokens that appear between verse lines or speaker turns in Shakespeare's dramatic texts

398.label: dialogue turn marker
398.description: Closing guillemet or end of one speech immediately preceding the opening of a new speech (speaker transition boundary in dramatic dialogue)

399.label: off/officer
399.description: The feature activates on the token 'off' (or the start of 'officer'), particularly in phrasal verb or particle constructions like 'cut off', 'cast off', 'break off', 'offered' (off-ered), appearing mid-sentence in Shakespearean dialogue.

400.label: speaker transition after sentence break
400.description: Detects the empty space/newline token immediately following a double-dash sentence boundary ('--') that marks a speaker change or new speech turn in dramatic dialogue

401.label: letter 'b' after hyphen or word boundary
401.description: The feature fires on the letter 'b' appearing at the start of a word component, particularly after hyphens or in compound words/hyphenated constructions

402.label: app- prefix
402.description: Tokens beginning with the substring 'app' (part of words like appointment, apprehension, apparition, apparel, apparent, appears, appliance, apple)

403.label: sentence-ending period
403.description: The feature fires on period punctuation marks that end sentences or clauses, particularly in mid-dialogue or mid-speech contexts in Shakespeare's works

404.label: coordinating conjunction in poetic/literary context
404.description: The feature fires on 'and' or 'doth' functioning as a conjunction or auxiliary verb connecting parallel elements within verse, particularly in rhythmic or list-like poetic constructions in Shakespearean text

405.label: letter 'p' mid-word boundary
405.description: Feature fires on the letter 'p' at the start of a stressed or content word following a space or word boundary, particularly in polysyllabic words beginning with 'p'

406.label: let him/them
406.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'him' or 'them' immediately following the word 'let', detecting the imperative construction 'let him/them [verb]'

407.label: titled nobility reference
407.description: The token is 'Duke' or 'King' appearing as a title referring to a specific nobleman or royal figure, typically preceded by 'the' and followed by a name or possessive

408.label: as well
408.description: The feature fires on the word 'well' in the phrase 'as well', used in comparative constructions meaning 'equally' or 'just as easily'

409.label: Negation response 'No'
409.description: The feature fires on the word 'No' when used as a direct negating response or refusal at the start of a speech or clause, often following a question or assertion

410.label: preposition starting prepositional phrase
410.description: Feature activates on preposition tokens (Like, From, With, In, Into) that begin a prepositional phrase following a clause or verb phrase

411.label: temporal/positional prepositions
411.description: The feature activates on prepositions indicating relative position or time, primarily 'before', with occasional 'which', 'either', and 'by', often in contexts of precedence or sequencing

412.label: fiend/fiends
412.description: The feature activates on the letter 'i' within the word 'fiend' or 'fiends' (and occasionally similar words like 'festivals', 'Epidamium'), most strongly when the word is 'fiend' in phrases like 'foul fiend' or 'fiend of hell'

413.label: prepositional phrase 'in [possessive/article] [noun]'
413.description: Detects the word 'in' (or similar preposition) followed by a determiner or possessive pronoun ('my', 'the', 'your') in a prepositional phrase, firing on the determiner/possessive token

414.label: end of verse line
414.description: Token occurs at or near the end of a verse line or rhyming position, often the final stressed word before a line break or punctuation boundary in iambic pentameter

415.label: post-interrogative pause token
415.description: Feature fires on the whitespace or token immediately following a question word (why, how, what, where, when) or interrogative phrase, capturing the position just after an interrogative pivot in dialogue

416.label: exclamation mark after dramatic/emphatic statement
416.description: The feature fires on '!' (and occasionally '.') punctuation marks that follow emotionally charged, exclamatory, or declamatory phrases in Shakespearean dramatic speech

417.label: the «e»
417.description: Feature fires on the letter 'e' at the start of a word that immediately follows the word 'the', capturing the bigram 'the e-' boundary

418.label: word-initial 's'
418.description: The feature fires on the letter 's' that begins a word, particularly after a space or word boundary, capturing word-initial 's' tokens in Shakespeare's text

419.label: adverb/adjective root form
419.description: The feature activates on base/root forms of words that have common adverbial or adjectival derivations (fit/fitly, dark/darkly, bold/boldly), appearing just before or as the stem without the suffix

420.label: infinitive 'to'
420.description: The feature activates on the word 'to' when it functions as an infinitive marker or directional preposition following an adjective, noun, or verb complement, introducing a purpose or action clause

421.label: possessive 's
421.description: The feature activates on the possessive clitic 's (apostrophe-s) indicating ownership or relationship, typically following a proper noun or common noun

422.label: imperative + pronoun object
422.description: Short imperative verb phrase followed immediately by a pronoun (him, you, them, it, both, not), typically in commands or urgent directives

423.label: pr/tr consonant cluster
423.description: The feature activates on the letter 'r' (or 'tr' digraph) when it immediately follows the consonant 'p' or 't', forming 'pr' or 'tr' consonant clusters at the start of syllables

424.label: never
424.description: The feature activates specifically on the word 'never', appearing in various syntactic contexts across Shakespeare's works

425.label: past participle adjective suffix
425.description: The feature activates on the 'ed' or 'ous' suffix of past participle or adjectival forms, particularly compound adjectives and participial modifiers (e.g., 'sealed', 'seasoned', 'enthroned', 'shrouded', 'dispersed')

426.label: end of verse line
426.description: Newline or whitespace token immediately following the end of a poetic verse line (often closing a rhyme or lyrical phrase in song/poetry)

427.label: line break after verb/predicate
427.description: Fires on the newline/space token immediately following a verb or predicate that ends a verse line, marking the enjambment or line-end boundary in iambic pentameter

428.label: like one
428.description: The feature activates on the word 'one' when used in simile or comparative constructions (e.g., 'like one', 'as one'), often introducing a figurative comparison or describing a singular entity in a metaphorical context.

429.label: subjunctive 'were'
429.description: The feature fires on the word 'were' used in a subjunctive or counterfactual conditional context (hypothetical, conditional, or contrary-to-fact constructions)

430.label: second person pronoun 'you' as object/indirect object
430.description: The feature fires on the word 'you' when it appears as an object or indirect object (not subject) of a verb or preposition, often in polite or deferential speech acts

431.label: second person object 'you' after verb
431.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'you' when it appears as a direct or indirect object immediately following a verb or preposition, functioning as the grammatical object of that verb phrase

432.label: war-related tokens
432.description: Tokens that are part of the word 'war' or compound words containing 'war' (war, warlike, warmth, warning, warring), often appearing in military or conflict contexts

433.label: love/men
433.description: The feature fires on the words 'love' and 'men' in Shakespearean text, capturing these two specific high-frequency thematic/lexical tokens

434.label: '-ious' suffix or 'poor'/'great' adjective
434.description: Adjectives ending in '-ious' or common adjectives like 'poor'/'great' used predicatively or attributively

435.label: abstract noun after 'to be' or similar copula
435.description: The feature fires on abstract nouns (nothing, ready, reason, matter) appearing after a linking/copula verb or auxiliary, often in short declarative or evaluative clauses

436.label: word starting with 'b'
436.description: The feature fires on tokens that are the letter 'b' at the start of a word, specifically the first character 'b' of words beginning with that letter

437.label: end of speech newline
437.description: The feature fires on the newline/whitespace character immediately following the closing punctuation of a speaker's line in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue, marking the boundary between one character's speech and the next speaker tag

438.label: there/therefore adverb
438.description: The feature fires on the word 'there' or 'therefore' (and contracted forms like 'thereon', 'there's', 'therein'), functioning as a locative or consequential adverb/conjunction at the start of or within a clause

439.label: newline/line-break token
439.description: The feature fires on whitespace or newline tokens that appear between speaker turns or at the end of a line of dialogue, marking scene/speech boundaries in dramatic text

440.label: speaker change newline
440.description: Whitespace or newline token immediately following a closing quotation or period that precedes a new character's dialogue tag in a play script

441.label: comma after proper name/title
441.description: The feature fires on commas that immediately follow a proper name, title, or honorific (person names, noble titles, place names) used in direct address or apposition

442.label: colon after clause
442.description: Feature fires on a colon (':') that follows a complete clause or phrase and introduces a continuation, explanation, or quoted speech

443.label: adjective 'good'/'fair' as modifier
443.description: The feature activates on the word 'good' (occasionally 'fair') when used as an attributive adjective directly modifying a noun, typically in phrases like 'good father', 'good repute', 'good aunt', 'good conceit', 'good youth', 'good will'

444.label: the word 'from'
444.description: Fires on the word 'from' in Shakespearean dramatic dialogue

445.label: coordinating conjunction 'and'
445.description: The feature fires on the word 'and' used as a coordinating conjunction joining two substantial phrases, clauses, or noun phrases in formal or elevated prose/verse contexts

446.label: imperative 'come'
446.description: The word 'come' used as an imperative or urgent call to action, often as a direct command or beckoning

447.label: preposition 'of' in partitive/genitive constructions
447.description: The feature fires on the word 'of' when it appears in partitive, genitive, or complement constructions, often following verbs like 'make', 'have', or 'be made', expressing composition, possession, or derivation

448.label: ag digraph
448.description: The feature activates on the letter sequence 'ag' within words, particularly in the middle of words containing the digraph 'ag' such as 'dagger', 'maggots', 'swagger', 'magistrate', 'propagation', 'wager'

449.label: Thou art
449.description: The feature detects the word 'art' when preceded by the second-person pronoun 'thou', identifying the archaic Shakespearean construction 'thou art' (meaning 'you are')

450.label: interrogative 'What' at start of speech
450.description: The feature fires on the word 'What' when it appears as the first word of a new speaker's line or exclamatory question in Shakespearean dialogue, typically marking surprise, inquiry, or a character's opening utterance

451.label: sh/st/ar/cont bigram onset
451.description: Feature activates on the first 2 characters of specific consonant cluster syllable onsets (sh, st, ar, cont) at word boundaries, likely detecting a phonological or subword tokenization pattern

452.label: word starting with 'm'
452.description: The feature fires on the first letter 'm' at the start of words beginning with 'm', particularly verbs like 'missed', 'mastered', 'mixed', 'mourn', 'maintain', 'mow', 'mingle'

453.label: ri- syllable onset
453.description: The feature fires on tokens beginning with the syllable 'ri' (or 'ri' as part of a stressed syllable onset), including words like 'ridiculous', 'rib', 'thrice', 'riches', 'richest', 'river', 'ripe', 'rigor', 'rid'

454.label: kinship/relational possessive noun
454.description: The feature fires on kinship or close relational nouns (brother, husband, master) that immediately follow a possessive determiner (my, your, his) referring to a personal relationship

455.label: any/every
455.description: The feature activates on the words 'any' and 'every', specifically these universal/existential determiner/pronoun forms in Shakespeare's text.

456.label: newline after punctuation
456.description: Feature activates on whitespace/newline characters that follow end-of-line punctuation or clause boundaries in verse, marking the start of a new line of dialogue or verse

457.label: word-final '-ed'/'-y'/'-et' suffixes in verse
457.description: Tokens that are word-final morphological suffixes (-ed, -y, -ny, -et, -ing) attached to a preceding stem, typically forming adjectives, nouns, or verb forms in Shakespearean verse lines

458.label: With at line/speech start
458.description: The word 'With' (or 'Within') appearing at the beginning of a new line or speech turn, typically following a speaker label or line break in the dramatic text

459.label: fa- prefix token
459.description: The feature detects the token fragment 'fa' as the beginning of words starting with 'fa' (faction, favors, fairest, faces), firing on this specific subword token in the context of Shakespearean text

460.label: en substring
460.description: Tokens containing the letters 'en' (often as a suffix or embedded sequence), such as '-en', '-ance', '-ent', '-men', '-risen', '-sudden', etc.

461.label: period ending short imperative or greeting utterance
461.description: Feature fires on period punctuation that closes a short sentence (often an imperative, greeting, or brief command) spoken by a character, typically followed immediately by a new speaker or clause

462.label: no more
462.description: The feature detects the phrase 'no more' (or 'once more'), specifically firing on the word 'more' when preceded by 'no' or 'once', often used as an emphatic dismissal or negation in Shakespearean dialogue

463.label: pronoun after semicolon or sentence boundary
463.description: The feature fires on 'it' or 'she' appearing at the start of a new clause or sentence, typically following a semicolon, period, or other clause boundary, functioning as a subject pronoun restarting discourse

464.label: pronoun/name after comma or clause boundary
464.description: The feature fires on 'that' or a proper name (Henry, Edward) appearing immediately after a punctuation mark or clause boundary, functioning as a discourse-level referential anchor introducing a new clause or direct address

465.label: Capitalized 'A' starting new clause/sentence
465.description: The feature fires on a capital letter 'A' that begins a new syntactic unit (sentence, clause, or line of verse), often following punctuation or a speaker label, functioning as the onset of a phrase beginning with 'A' (articles, prepositions, or words like 'Admit', 'Advance', 'Accountant', 'Appear').

466.label: blank line / stanza break
466.description: Whitespace or empty line token marking a line break or stanza boundary in verse or song

467.label: "You" at start of new speech turn
467.description: The feature fires on the token 'You' (or 'You' beginning 'Young') when it appears as the first word of a new speaker's line in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue, i.e. at the start of a character's utterance following a speech prefix or sentence boundary.

468.label: at_substring
468.description: Tokens containing the substring 'at' (case-insensitive), typically mid-word occurrences of the letter sequence 'at'

469.label: interrogative echo phrase
469.description: Feature activates on 'think' or 'say' immediately following 'What', forming the rhetorical question pattern 'What think/say you/est thou'

470.label: function word after verb/clause
470.description: Feature activates on common function words ('like', 'begin', 'how') that appear immediately after a verb or at the start of a new clause or comparative/manner construction

471.label: comparative 'than'/'of'
471.description: Tokens 'than' or 'of' appearing in comparative constructions, often following 'more' or similar comparatives

472.label: Opening quotation mark
472.description: The feature fires on the opening double quotation mark character '"' that begins a direct quote or quoted phrase in Shakespeare's text

473.label: Capitalized H at speech/sentence boundary
473.description: The feature fires on capital letter 'H' appearing at the start of a new utterance, exclamation, or dramatic speech turn in Shakespearean dialogue, often following stage direction breaks or sentence boundaries

474.label: past participle verb form
474.description: Tokens that are past participle verb endings (-ed, -ked, -ged, -ced, -bed, -ned) split mid-word at a morpheme boundary, often the '-ed' suffix of past participles in verb phrases

475.label: contraction apostrophe-s
475.description: The feature fires on the 's in contractions like 'That's' and 'Where's', capturing the possessive or copula contraction token following a demonstrative or interrogative pronoun in Shakespeare's dialogue

476.label: adversative/temporal clause opener
476.description: The feature activates on 'But' or 'When' at the start of a subordinate or contrasting clause, typically following a completed thought or main clause, marking a turn or temporal condition in the sentence structure

477.label: sil/ran prefix
477.description: Fires on tokens beginning with 'sil' (as in silver, silk, silence, silent) or occasionally similar soft consonant clusters, predominantly the 'sil' morpheme appearing at the start of words related to silver, silk, or silence

478.label: character cue response
478.description: Fires on the token immediately following a character name/speaker label (VALENTINE, PROTEUS, CELIA, SPEED) that introduces their dialogue response in a back-and-forth exchange

479.label: preposition 'for'
479.description: The feature activates on the word 'for' used as a preposition in various syntactic contexts throughout Shakespeare's works

480.label: O, exclamatory vocative
480.description: The feature detects the exclamatory interjection 'O' followed by a comma, marking rhetorical apostrophe or emotional outburst in Shakespearean verse

481.label: proper noun followed by line break or continuation
481.description: Feature fires on whitespace or newline tokens that immediately follow a proper noun (name, title, or foreign word) at a phrase or clause boundary

482.label: well_response
482.description: The word 'Well' used as a discourse marker or response at the start of a character's spoken line in Shakespearean dialogue

483.label: Sentence-initial pronoun subject
483.description: Third-person pronoun (It/He/She) appearing at the start of a new clause or sentence, often after a line break or punctuation boundary, functioning as the grammatical subject

484.label: speaker label prefix
484.description: The feature activates on the beginning of a speaker label (character name) that serves as a dialogue attribution in the play script, particularly 'MISTRESS' and partial name tokens at the start of a speaking turn

485.label: royal/collective 'our'
485.description: The feature detects the possessive pronoun 'our' used in a royal or political collective context, particularly by monarchs or nobles speaking on behalf of a group or in the royal we construction

486.label: first-person present tense verb phrase
486.description: Feature activates on the main verb immediately following the subject pronoun 'I', capturing short first-person declarative or performative verb constructions (e.g., 'I see', 'I pray', 'I do', 'I thank', 'I could', 'I saw')

487.label: Quoted speech starting with A
487.description: The feature fires on the letter 'A' at the beginning of a word that appears inside quotation marks, i.e. the first character of a directly quoted word or phrase beginning with A.

488.label: Rhetorical 'How' question opener
488.description: The feature fires on the word 'How' when it begins a rhetorical or exclamatory question, typically mid-discourse expressing wonder, scorn, or emotional intensity

489.label: possessive 'my/your/thy lord'
489.description: The feature fires on the word 'lord' when immediately preceded by a possessive pronoun (my, your, thy) or used as a direct address/reference with a possessive determiner, indicating a feudal or respectful possessive relationship to a lord

490.label: speaker_turn_marker
490.description: Fires on the whitespace/empty token immediately following a period or end of speech that precedes a new character name (stage direction speaker label), marking the transition between dialogue turns in Shakespeare's dramatic texts

491.label: -ly adverb/adjective suffix
491.description: The feature activates on the 'ly' token that forms the end of words with the '-ly' suffix, including adverbs (cowardly, softly, solemnly, briefly, lately, safely, grievingly, clerkly, overboldly, only) and adjectives (kingly, princely)

492.label: so (conjunctive/adverbial)
492.description: The word 'so' used as a conjunction or adverbial connector linking clauses or sentences, often at or near the start of a clause

493.label: comparative 'than' or 'se' digraph
493.description: Fires on the word 'Than' used in comparative constructions (X more/less than Y) or on the digraph 'se' as part of words like 'sell', 'sepulcher', 'season'

494.label: feminine pronoun object/subject in dialogue
494.description: The feature fires on 'her' or 'she' when used as a pronoun referring to a female character in conversational/dialogue exchanges, particularly in contexts involving pursuit, arrangement, or discussion of a woman

495.label: imperative_comma_and
495.description: Token immediately following a short imperative verb ('Go', 'Come') at a clause boundary, specifically the comma or conjunction (',' or 'and') that follows the imperative before a new clause or command begins

496.label: coordinating conjunction 'and'
496.description: The feature fires on the word 'and' used as a coordinating conjunction joining two clauses, phrases, or noun phrases, particularly where 'and' connects parallel or contrasting elements mid-sentence

497.label: short function word after verb/auxiliary
497.description: Feature fires on short function words (no, a, his, the, did) that appear immediately after a verb, auxiliary verb, or similar grammatical head in mid-clause positions

498.label: en- prefix words
498.description: Tokens that are the 'en' prefix morpheme of English words beginning with 'en' (envy, enjoy, enamored, engage, enforce, enrich, ensnare, enough, etc.)

499.label: speaker_transition_newline
499.description: Detects the whitespace/newline character between a closing speech line and the next speaker label in dramatic dialogue (The Tempest), marking the boundary between one character's utterance and the next character's name

500.label: complementizer 'that'
500.description: The feature detects the word 'that' functioning as a complementizer introducing a subordinate clause, typically following verbs of communication, cognition, or reporting (e.g., 'tell him that', 'he told me that', 'thinks that')

501.label: ent/att substring
501.description: The feature activates on the character sequence 'ent' (or occasionally 'att'/'t') appearing as part of words, particularly in words like 'enthroned', 'entered', 'entrance', 'entrails', 'entrenched', 'entombed', capturing this specific orthographic substring at word-internal boundaries

502.label: pre/bu syllable break
502.description: The feature fires on the second character of words split across a line or token boundary, specifically the letters 'pre' or 'bu' at the start of a new token where the word began on the previous token (e.g., 'pre'vented, 'pre'ferment, 'bu'ried)

503.label: of + noun phrase
503.description: The feature activates on the word 'of' when it appears as a preposition linking a preceding noun to a following noun phrase, typically in formal or poetic constructions expressing possession, composition, or association

504.label: speaker label followed by newline
504.description: The feature fires on the newline/space character immediately following a character name/speaker label in Shakespeare dramatic dialogue, marking the transition from speaker attribution to spoken text

505.label: comma after speaker tag or conjunction
505.description: Feature activates on commas that immediately follow a speaker attribution label (e.g., 'MISTRESS QUICKLY,') or a conjunctive transition word (e.g., 'But,', 'And,') in Shakespearean dramatic dialogue

506.label: Capital F mid-sentence
506.description: The feature fires on the capital letter 'F' at the start of a word that begins a new clause or line of verse, but appears mid-sentence (not at the absolute start of a sentence), typically following punctuation or a line break in Shakespeare's verse or prose.

507.label: t-initial stressed syllable
507.description: The feature fires on the letter 't' at the start of a stressed or content word (verb, noun, or adjective), particularly where 't' begins a semantically significant word following a word boundary or apostrophe clitic

508.label: night
508.description: The token 'night' in various temporal or atmospheric contexts across Shakespeare's works

509.label: speaker label after stage direction
509.description: Token immediately following a closing speaker/stage tag, specifically the newline or space character that precedes a new character's spoken line in dramatic dialogue

510.label: verb 'do'
510.description: The feature activates on the word 'do' (present tense form of the verb 'to do') in various syntactic contexts across Shakespeare's works

511.label: line break after 'you'
511.description: The feature fires on a whitespace or newline character that immediately follows the word 'you', marking a mid-sentence line or verse break after 'you'

512.label: third-person masculine pronoun 'he'
512.description: The feature fires on the word 'he' (or 'He') when used as a third-person masculine subject pronoun, typically referring to a previously mentioned male character

513.label: speech/dialogue interruption or pause marker
513.description: Feature activates on whitespace or punctuation (em-dash, space) immediately following a speaker label or mid-sentence interruption, marking transitions or breaks in dramatic dialogue

514.label: auxiliary verb after subject pronoun or noun
514.description: Feature fires on forms of 'be' (was/being/tis) that appear as auxiliary or copular verbs immediately following a subject, often in embedded or subordinate clauses

515.label: al- prefix or FALSTAFF speaker tag
515.description: Fires on tokens beginning with 'al' (as part of words like 'alteration', 'altered', 'alarum', 'alarms') and on the speaker label 'FALSTAFF' at the start of a speech

516.label: Will/Shall you/I question
516.description: The feature fires on the pronoun immediately following 'Will' or 'Shall' in direct interrogative constructions (e.g., 'Will «you»', 'Shall «I»'), capturing this specific syntactic pattern of modal verb + subject pronoun in questions.

517.label: HAMLET speaker tag
517.description: The feature fires on the token 'HAMLET' (or similar character name tags like 'COST' in COSTARD) when they appear as a speaker label introducing a new line of dialogue, typically following another character's speech.

518.label: '-at' word endings
518.description: The feature fires on the 'at' token in words ending with the '-at' sound/spelling, such as 'heat', 'throat', 'boat', 'coat', 'flat', 'beat'

519.label: character speech onset
519.description: Fires on the first token (often a space or quotation mark) immediately after a character name/speaker label, marking the start of a new character's spoken line in dramatic dialogue

520.label: comma after noun/adjective phrase
520.description: Feature fires on commas that follow a noun, adjective, or short phrase mid-clause, typically marking list items or appositive/parenthetical boundaries in poetic or dramatic prose

521.label: capital C word at line/speech start
521.description: The feature fires on the letter 'C' when it begins a new speech, sentence, or clause at the start of a line, particularly on character names (CYMBELINE, CLIFFORD, CITIZEN, CLARENCE) or content words (Could, Caesar, Cover, Corruption, Continue, Couch) that open a new utterance after a period or line break

522.label: comma after noun phrase
522.description: Feature activates on commas that follow a noun phrase or list item, typically mid-clause after a subject, object, or appositive construction

523.label: dash or period followed by new clause
523.description: Feature fires on a space or newline immediately following a punctuation mark (period, dash, or comma) that ends one clause/sentence and precedes a new direct address or clause, functioning as a clause boundary separator in dramatic dialogue

524.label: letter 'p' after space or mid-word
524.description: The feature activates on the letter 'p' (lowercase), particularly at the start of a word or syllable boundary, spanning various syntactic and thematic contexts

525.label: reflexive/intensifying pronoun or adverb mid-clause
525.description: Tokens that are reflexive pronouns (itself, yourself), degree adverbs (much), or similar intensifiers/reflexives appearing mid-clause after a verb or preposition

526.label: ol/oll substring
526.description: The feature fires on tokens containing the substring 'ol' or 'oll', particularly within longer words (e.g., 'Neapolitan', 'politic', 'unpolished', 'metropolis', 'Tripolis', 'Bardolph', 'Parolles')

527.label: second person possessive 'thy'
527.description: The feature fires on the archaic second person possessive pronoun 'thy' in Shakespearean text

528.label: proper noun referent
528.description: The feature activates on proper nouns referring to authority/father figures (Caesar or father), typically appearing after a possessive or relational context

529.label: speaker label follows
529.description: Newline/paragraph break immediately before a character name/speaker label in Shakespeare dramatic text

530.label: Proper noun starting with P
530.description: The feature activates on the letter 'P' that begins a proper noun (character name or place name) following a title, address, or narrative context

531.label: Capitalized word after quotation/boundary
531.description: The feature activates on capitalized letters that begin a word immediately following a closing quotation mark, a period, or a line/speech boundary, marking the start of a new utterance or quoted phrase

532.label: Capitalized T proper noun
532.description: The feature fires on the letter 'T' at the start of capitalized proper nouns or personifications, including names like Talbot, Tarquin, Tarsus, and the personified noun Time

533.label: mid-word or word-final morpheme
533.description: Feature fires on tokens that are suffixes or word-internal/final fragments completing a word, often inflectional endings (-eth, -ings, -s) or the tail portion of a word split across tokens

534.label: are (copula)
534.description: The feature fires on the word 'are' functioning as a linking/copular verb, typically in the pattern '[noun phrase] are [predicate]'

535.label: sp- word onset
535.description: The feature fires on the token 'sp' at the beginning of words starting with 'sp' (such as 'spend', 'spy', 'spare', 'spell', 'sparingly'), capturing the onset of this specific consonant cluster in Shakespeare's text.

536.label: body part as subject of relative/possessive clause
536.description: The feature fires on body part nouns (eye, hand, woe, sea, arms) that appear as the head noun in a possessive or relative clause construction, typically preceded by 'whose' or a possessive determiner

537.label: interrogative 'Why'
537.description: The feature fires on the word 'Why' used as a question or exclamation at the start of a spoken line or dialogue turn in Shakespeare

538.label: exclamation mark
538.description: The feature fires on exclamation points (!) and occasionally strong punctuation marks at the end of emotionally charged or emphatic utterances in Shakespeare

539.label: negation marker 'not'
539.description: The feature fires on the word 'not' in various syntactic contexts (imperatives, questions, statements) across Shakespeare's works

540.label: acc- prefix or 'de' article
540.description: Tokens that are the beginning of words starting with 'acc' (account, according, accent, accuse, accursed, accidental) or the French article/particle 'de'

541.label: comma after repeated/exclamatory word or name
541.description: Fires on commas that immediately follow a vocative name, interjection, or repeated word in an emotional or exclamatory context

542.label: third-person singular present tense verb
542.description: Verb inflected for third-person singular present tense (grows, stands, draws, finds), typically following a singular noun or pronoun subject

543.label: rem/J word fragment
543.description: Feature activates on tokens that are the beginning fragment of words starting with 'rem' (remain, remove, remarkable, remorse, removed) or the letter 'J' as a word/name initial, appearing mid-sentence in Shakespeare text

544.label: preposition/conjunction followed by line break or continuation
544.description: Feature fires on whitespace or newline tokens that immediately follow a preposition, conjunction, or functional word, bridging a syntactic boundary mid-phrase

545.label: past participle morpheme boundary
545.description: The feature fires on the boundary within past participle or past tense verb forms, specifically at the junction where a prefix/root meets a suffix (e.g., 'be-headed', 'be-witched', 'be-reft', 'cra-zed', 'ra-zed', 'am-bitious', 'pro-voked', 'be-opened', 'be-hatched'), capturing the morphological split point inside inflected or derived verb forms

546.label: question mark after dialogue/speech
546.description: The feature fires on '?' tokens that appear at the end of rhetorical or direct questions within spoken dialogue in Shakespeare's plays

547.label: modal/auxiliary 'have' in conditional or counterfactual context
547.description: The feature fires on the word 'have' when it appears as part of a modal or auxiliary construction, particularly in conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical clauses (e.g., 'should have', 'would have', 'I have')

548.label: cur/wor substring
548.description: The feature fires on tokens that are the beginning of words containing the substring 'cur' (current, curb, curse, curtsy, cur) or occasionally similar phonetic patterns like 'wor', detecting this specific orthographic/phonological fragment at the start of a token

549.label: closing quotation mark
549.description: Closing double quotation mark ending a directly quoted word, phrase, or passage

550.label: I am
550.description: The feature detects the phrase 'I am' spoken by a character in Shakespearean dialogue, specifically firing on the token 'am' following 'I' in first-person declarations or responses.

551.label: sp- prefix fragment
551.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the 'sp' fragment at the start of a word beginning with 'sp' (spits, spur, spring, space, spare, spurs, sphere, spectacle, sphere, spectatorship, spite), appearing mid-word after a line break or space in the tokenization.

552.label: im- prefix words
552.description: Tokens that are the 'im' portion of words beginning with the prefix 'im-' (imagination, imperious, immodest, imperceiverant, image, images, immediate, immanity, imperial, etc.)

553.label: br/gr digraph
553.description: The feature fires on tokens starting with the digraph 'br' or 'gr', capturing the onset of words beginning with these consonant clusters

554.label: see
554.description: The word 'see' (or its inflected form 'seeing') used in various syntactic positions across Shakespeare's works

555.label: temporal 'when' clause
555.description: The feature fires on the word 'when' introducing a temporal subordinate clause, typically following a main clause or narrative setup

556.label: Capital F after guillemets
556.description: The feature fires on the token 'F' (capital F) that appears at the start of a new speech or sentence, typically following a speaker attribution or punctuation, representing the first letter of words like 'Fair', 'Faith', 'Fie', 'Fear', 'Few', 'Full', 'Frame', 'First', 'Foh'

557.label: comma after imperative/vocative
557.description: Feature fires on commas that follow imperative verbs (Come, Go) or vocative addresses (sir, boy, lords, mother), typically marking a pause or direct address in commands and exclamations

558.label: second person pronoun 'thou' as subject/object
558.description: The feature fires on the word 'thou' in Early Modern English contexts, detecting this archaic second person singular pronoun regardless of its syntactic role

559.label: Naples/Nap
559.description: The feature detects the token 'Nap' as part of the word 'Naples', a proper noun referring to the city/kingdom of Naples in Shakespeare's The Tempest

560.label: Character name as speech prefix
560.description: The feature activates on character names (predominantly WARWICK, also PRINCE EDWARD) appearing as dramatic speech prefixes that introduce a new speaker's dialogue in the play

561.label: lord (address/reference)
561.description: The feature fires on the word 'lord' when used as a title of address or reference to a superior, typically preceded by a possessive or modifier (my lord, our lord, blunt-witted lord)

562.label: short function word after imperative/verb phrase
562.description: Feature fires on short common words (soon, it, you, death, die, to, fast) that appear immediately after a verb or imperative phrase, typically in short clause-final or post-verbal positions

563.label: speaker attribution newline
563.description: Whitespace or newline immediately following a closing guillemet that marks the end of a speech line and precedes a new speaker tag in Shakespearean dramatic text

564.label: temporal 'then'
564.description: The word 'then' used as a temporal or logical connector indicating sequence or consequence

565.label: '-ut-' or '-ang-' infix cluster
565.description: Fires on the letters 'ut' or 'ang' appearing as an internal substring within a word, capturing these specific letter sequences embedded mid-token (e.g. 'unt-ang-led', 'glut', 'persec-ut-ion', 'rep-ut-ed', 'd-ang-ers')

566.label: Capitalized M mid-sentence
566.description: The feature activates on uppercase letter 'M' appearing at the start of a word in the middle of a sentence (not at sentence start), typically after punctuation like commas, semicolons, or periods that begin a new clause

567.label: speaker transition newline
567.description: The feature fires on the newline/whitespace character that immediately follows a character's spoken line and precedes the next speaker label in dramatic dialogue

568.label: fl- sequence
568.description: The feature fires on the letters 'fl' appearing together, detecting this specific two-character sequence (as in 'flies', 'flowers', 'flight', 'flat', 'flames', 'flatterers', 'flags') regardless of context, theme, or syntax.

569.label: demonstrative 'this'
569.description: The feature fires on the word 'this' used as a demonstrative determiner or pronoun in Shakespearean text, pointing to a nearby referent

570.label: ex- prefix
570.description: The feature activates on the token 'ex' when it begins a word with the Latin prefix 'ex-' (exile, exhibit, expect, excess, exclaim, exclaimed, exiled, exclaims)

571.label: sentence-ending period after complete clause
571.description: Fires on periods that end complete syntactic clauses or sentences in dialogue/prose, particularly after direct speech or narrative statements by named characters

572.label: preposition 'at'/'doth'/'is' after verb phrase
572.description: The feature fires on short function words ('at', 'doth', 'is') that follow a verb or verb phrase, typically in a verb + preposition/auxiliary construction within a clause

573.label: hyphen or space in compound/hyphenated word
573.description: The feature fires on hyphens or spaces that appear mid-compound, connecting parts of hyphenated compound words or compound expressions (e.g., 'lily-livered', 'far-off', 'foul-mouthed', 'five-and-twenty', 'a-wooing', 'a-ripening')

574.label: short speaker response
574.description: A character's spoken line begins with a very short utterance (typically 2-5 words) immediately following the speaker label, often a brief reaction, question, or exclamation

575.label: demonstrative 'this'
575.description: The feature fires on the word 'this' used as a demonstrative pronoun or determiner, typically in interrogative or declarative constructions pointing to something contextually present

576.label: newline/paragraph break after punctuation
576.description: Feature fires on whitespace or newline tokens that follow end-of-sentence punctuation (period, exclamation mark, or question mark) marking a speaker turn or sentence boundary in dramatic dialogue

577.label: exclamation mark after short imperative/exclamatory phrase
577.description: Feature activates on '!' tokens that follow urgent commands, exclamations, or emotional outbursts, typically in high-intensity dramatic speech

578.label: comma after clause boundary
578.description: Feature fires on commas that follow a complete clause or phrase, typically marking a syntactic boundary between two independent or semi-independent clauses

579.label: sp/ast fragment
579.description: Fires on the letters 'sp' or 'ast' as part of words, specifically detecting the digraph/trigraph pattern where 's' is followed by 'p' or 'as' is followed by 't' at a token boundary mid-word

580.label: time/place adverbials
580.description: Temporal or spatial adverbial nouns indicating time of day, location, or relative time (night, day, home, hence, after, land)

581.label: newline after line break
581.description: Feature fires on a space or newline character that follows a line break or verse boundary in Shakespeare's text, typically at the junction between verse lines or between a speech and its continuation

582.label: possessive/attributive adjective before noun
582.description: Feature fires on possessive pronoun 'thy' or attributive adjectives like 'sweet' and 'royal' appearing directly before a noun in a noun phrase

583.label: ha- words
583.description: The feature activates on the token 'ha' as the beginning of words starting with 'ha' (hazards, haughty, hawthorn, hammer, hap, habiliments, haunch, hawks)

584.label: end of exclamatory or declarative speech line
584.description: Feature fires on sentence-ending punctuation (! or .) that closes a character's spoken line in a Shakespeare play, typically at the boundary between one character's speech and the next speaker's cue

585.label: reflexive possessive 'own'
585.description: The feature fires on the word 'own' when used as a reflexive possessive adjective (your own, his own, mine own, our own), emphasizing personal ownership or self-reference

586.label: exclamatory 'What,' or transitional 'Now,'
586.description: The feature fires on a comma immediately following 'What' or 'Now' used as a discourse-level interjection or attention-getter at the start of a speech or clause, signaling surprise, indignation, or a conversational pivot

587.label: pl/mon/ac syllable onset
587.description: Tokens that are the beginning of words starting with consonant clusters 'pl', 'mon', or 'ac' (multi-consonant or consonant+vowel onsets in stressed syllables)

588.label: est substring
588.description: The feature activates on tokens containing the substring 'est', including words like 'earnest', 'manifest', 'harvest', 'earliest', 'tempest', 'honest', 'blessed', 'worthiest', 'seest'

589.label: as
589.description: The feature detects the letters 'as' appearing within a token, including standalone word 'as', the suffix '-as' in names like 'Thomas', and the substring 'as' in words like 'pleasant', 'greasy', 'Whereas', 'Dashing'

590.label: line break / verse newline
590.description: The feature fires on whitespace or empty tokens that represent a line break between verse lines in Shakespeare's poetry

591.label: This is
591.description: The feature detects the phrase 'This is' used as a presentational or declarative construction, where a speaker introduces, identifies, or asserts something, typically at or near a clause boundary.

592.label: character_name_speech_start
592.description: Activates on the whitespace/newline token immediately following a character name label (e.g. HAMLET, URSULA, CORNWALL) that introduces a new line of dialogue in a Shakespeare play script

593.label: speech prefix 'To'
593.description: The word 'To' appearing at the start of a character's spoken line in Shakespeare dialogue, immediately following a character name/speaker label

594.label: MESSAL/MESSALA
594.description: The feature detects the token fragment 'AL' (and once 'UN') as part of the character name 'MESSALA' (and once 'EDMUND') in Shakespeare speaker labels, specifically firing on the middle syllable of these proper names in dramatic speech prefixes.

595.label: hyphenated compound adjective
595.description: Detects the hyphen character within hyphenated compound words/adjectives (e.g. well-steeled, death-worthy, free-footed, soft-hearted), firing specifically on the hyphen token that joins two words into a compound modifier

596.label: '-ish' suffix or similar word-final morpheme
596.description: The feature activates on the letters 'ish' (or similar endings like 'ide') appearing at the end of words, capturing a specific sub-word morphological pattern including the suffix '-ish' and occasionally other word-final sequences

597.label: First-person pronoun 'I' after clause boundary
597.description: The feature fires on the word 'I' (or 'I' as part of a contraction/capitalized start) when it appears at or near the beginning of a new clause or sentence, often following punctuation or a subordinating conjunction, marking a shift to first-person agency in speech

598.label: end of speech marker
598.description: The feature fires on the closing guillemet or newline/space token that immediately follows the end of a character's speech, just before the next speaker label appears

599.label: first-person possessive pronoun
599.description: The feature activates on the archaic/poetic possessive pronouns 'mine' and 'thine' used as determiners or standalone possessives in Shakespearean dialogue

600.label: letter p after word break
600.description: The feature fires on the letter 'p' when it occurs at a morpheme or syllable boundary within a word, particularly where a prefix, compound junction, or syllable split places 'p' at the start of a new morpheme (e.g., cod-piece, tad-pole, tres-pass, inter-preter, tro-phies, envelo-p)

601.label: Period ending speech
601.description: A period (full stop) that concludes a complete spoken utterance or sentence, typically at the end of a character's dialogue turn before a new speaker or continuation

602.label: im substring
602.description: Fires on the letters 'im' appearing as a substring within longer words, particularly in word-internal positions (e.g., timber, pilgrim, testimony, timeless, impediment, pusillanimity, patrimony, pilgrimage, compliments)

603.label: comma after phrase/clause
603.description: The feature fires on commas that follow a noun phrase, verb phrase, or prepositional phrase, typically marking a syntactic boundary mid-clause or mid-list in verse or prose

604.label: relative clause introducer 'That'
604.description: The feature fires on the word 'That' when it introduces a relative clause or result clause following a noun or adjective phrase, functioning as a subordinating conjunction or relative pronoun linking a dependent clause to the preceding main clause

605.label: end of short utterance
605.description: Punctuation mark (period or exclamation mark) ending a short, complete dramatic utterance or sentence spoken by a character, typically fewer than 6 words

606.label: off/off- prefix
606.description: The feature activates on the token 'off' whether as a standalone word or as the beginning of words prefixed with 'off-' (e.g., 'officer', 'officious', 'offered')

607.label: archaic 'an' conjunction
607.description: The token 'an' used as an archaic/Early Modern English conjunction meaning 'if' or 'and', appearing in conditional or additive clauses in Shakespearean text

608.label: the word 'one'
608.description: Fires on the word 'one' used as a pronoun or numeral in various syntactic contexts

609.label: verb 'say' mid-utterance
609.description: The feature fires on the word 'say' (or its inflected form 'sayst') when it appears as a speech-act or reporting verb within dialogue, typically in the middle of a sentence or clause where a character is asserting, reporting, or quoting speech.

610.label: indefinite article 'a'
610.description: The feature fires on the indefinite article 'a' appearing mid-sentence, typically before a noun phrase

611.label: affirmative/responsive interjection followed by comma
611.description: The feature fires on a comma that immediately follows a short affirmative or responsive interjection ('Ay', 'Faith', 'Do', 'No had') at the start of a character's speech turn, capturing the pattern of dialogue-opening filler words punctuated by a comma.

612.label: Capitalized T word
612.description: The feature fires on capitalized words beginning with the letter 'T', including proper nouns like 'Talbot', 'Tripoli', 'Tubal', 'Trebonius', 'Tartars', and common nouns used with personification like 'Time'

613.label: ic substring
613.description: The feature fires on the letter sequence 'ic' appearing within words, particularly in words like 'policy', 'prophetic', 'physic', and 'relic'

614.label: The at clause boundary
614.description: The definite article 'The' appearing at the start of a new syntactic clause or sentence, often following a semicolon, comma, or line break, introducing a continuation or contrast in verse or prose

615.label: gra- prefix
615.description: Tokens beginning with 'gra' (graces, grapes, grander, grandparent, graver, grandsire, etc.)

616.label: eas/n/itt substring
616.description: The feature activates on partial tokens that are substrings of words, specifically the token fragments 'eas', 'n', or 'itt' appearing mid-word, predominantly 'eas' as part of words like 'easy' or 'easier'

617.label: comma after list/clause
617.description: Feature fires on commas that follow enumerated items, names, or embedded clauses within a larger sentence, typically mid-sentence after a noun phrase or short clause ending a list or parenthetical

618.label: speech act boundary newline token
618.description: Detects the newline or whitespace token immediately following a character name/speaker label in Shakespeare dramatic dialogue, marking the transition from speaker attribution to spoken text

619.label: comma at end of line
619.description: The feature fires on commas that appear at the end of a verse line or clause boundary, typically preceding a line break in Shakespeare's verse

620.label: word starting with 'v'
620.description: The feature fires on tokens that are the letter 'v' beginning a word, specifically at the start of words beginning with 'v' such as 'vows', 'vaunting', 'variation', 'vasty', 'valleys', 'vulgar', 'vapor', 'vicar', 'virginity', 'venerable', 'vowels', 'volume'

621.label: word ending in 'y'
621.description: The feature fires on the letter 'y' that completes a word, particularly adjectives, adverbs, or other words ending in the suffix '-y' (e.g., 'thirsty', 'rosy', 'wealthy', 'hasty', 'royally', 'bandy', 'heresy', 'canopy')

622.label: double-d spelling
622.description: The feature fires on the letter 'd' that forms part of a 'dd' or '-d' suffix pattern, particularly in words where a 'd' follows another 'd' or appears as part of past-tense/participial endings like '-dded', '-dden', or in words containing 'dd' clusters such as 'shrewd', 'sudden', 'misdoubt', 'bedded', 'gunpowder', 'scared', 'dared', 'Order', 'Macduff', 'giddy', 'trod', 'Saddled'

623.label: est/est-containing syllable
623.description: The feature fires on tokens containing the substring 'est', particularly the 'est' portion of words like 'testimonies', 'givest', 'Domestic', 'suggests', 'modest', 'Bestial', 'lest', 'Happiest', 'Destroying', 'busiest', 'request'

624.label: God [bless/save/give/den]
624.description: The feature fires on the word 'God' when it begins a pious greeting or blessing phrase (e.g. 'God save the King', 'God bless you', 'God give you good morrow'), typically spoken as a salutation by a character.

625.label: world
625.description: The token 'world' (or its morphological variants like 'world-sharers', 'world's', 'worlds') appearing in Shakespearean text

626.label: forth (phrasal verb complement)
626.description: The word 'forth' appearing as part of imperative or directional phrasal constructions (bring forth, call forth, come forth, draw forth, lead forth, send forth, lay forth, take forth)

627.label: en- prefix
627.description: Tokens beginning with the prefix 'en-' (enacted, enamored, enforce, enfranchised, enforced, enacted, envy, enjoyed, enfranchised, enow, envy, envy)

628.label: letter 's'
628.description: The feature fires on the letter 's' at the start of a word or within a word, particularly where 's' begins a syllable or word

629.label: sh- onset
629.description: The feature fires on tokens beginning with the letters 'sh', capturing words like shore, shrewd, shrew, shine, shorter, shut, shrift, shirt, shrouding, shuffling, shuttle, etc.

630.label: you after verb
630.description: The word 'you' appearing immediately after a verb, especially in direct address or interrogative constructions like 'Know you', 'But you', 'If you', 'I think you'

631.label: an digraph in proper nouns/Latin words
631.description: The feature fires on the letters 'an' appearing within words, particularly in proper nouns like 'Dunsinane', 'Milan', 'Satan', and Latin/foreign terms like 'tanquam', often mid-word

632.label: conjunction/pronoun after punctuation
632.description: Feature activates on conjunctions (who, though, and, if) or function words that appear immediately after a sentence boundary or clause boundary, often introducing a new clause or question

633.label: post-comma/post-punctuation space
633.description: Fires on space tokens that immediately follow a comma, period, or other punctuation mark, typically at a dialogue or clause boundary in dramatic text

634.label: Capital L
634.description: The feature fires on the uppercase letter 'L' at the start of a word, regardless of context, theme, or syntax

635.label: possessive pronoun 'thine'/'mine'
635.description: The feature fires on the archaic possessive pronouns 'thine' and 'mine' when used as pre-nominal or post-nominal possessives in Shakespearean text

636.label: Character speech tag
636.description: The feature fires on the space or token immediately following a character name/speaker label (in all caps) that introduces a new line of dialogue

637.label: inverted question with 'are/were you'
637.description: The feature fires on 'you' or 'not' immediately following the verb 'are' or 'were' in direct questions addressed to a character, typically in the form 'Are you...?' or 'Were you...?'

638.label: conjunction or comma in list/enumeration context
638.description: Feature fires on conjunctions ('and', 'but') and commas that connect names, titles, or items in a series or paired constructions within historical/political discourse

639.label: wit/witch token
639.description: Fires on the token 'wit' which begins words like 'wit', 'witch', 'withered', 'wits' — capturing the orthographic sequence 'wit' at the start of a word

640.label: in the
640.description: Feature activates on 'the' when immediately preceded by the preposition 'in' or 'on', forming common prepositional phrases

641.label: past participle adjective ending in -ed
641.description: Tokens that are the '-ed' suffix completing a past participle used adjectivally or in passive constructions, often mid-word after the stem

642.label: word-initial 'r' after apostrophe/space boundary
642.description: Fires on the letter 'r' at the start of a word, particularly words beginning with 'r' such as 'ransom', 'rough', 'rise', 'roof', 'rounds', 'rising', 'rate' — detecting word-initial 'r' tokens in Shakespearean text

643.label: Was/wash capitalized mid-sentence
643.description: The feature activates on the word 'Was' (or 'Wash') appearing in the middle of a sentence, typically as the past tense of 'to be' following a clause boundary, comma, or subordinate clause

644.label: infinitive/directional 'to'
644.description: The word 'to' functioning as part of an infinitive phrase or directional/dative preposition following a verb or noun, connecting an action or object to its target or purpose

645.label: preposition 'by' in passive/agentive constructions
645.description: The feature fires on the word 'by' when it functions as an agentive or instrumental preposition, typically following a passive verb form or indicating means/agency in a clause

646.label: end of line/verse boundary
646.description: Newline or verse boundary token (blank space) occurring immediately after the end of a line of verse or speech, marking the transition between one speaker's line and the next

647.label: possessive_pronoun_sentence_start
647.description: Fires on first-person or second-person possessive pronouns ('My', 'Our', 'Thy') that appear at the start of a new clause or sentence following a sentence boundary (period, exclamation mark, or strong syntactic break)

648.label: pronoun/short-word after verb or punctuation
648.description: Feature activates on short function words (pronouns, adverbs, punctuation) that immediately follow a verb or comma, typically within a syntactic unit involving a verb and its object or complement

649.label: verb or adverb in mid-sentence position
649.description: The feature activates on words that function as verbs, adverbs, or verb-adjacent particles appearing mid-clause, often following a subject or auxiliary, including words like 'rest', 'still', 'fast', 'before', 'pleasure', 'act', 'assist', 'presently'

650.label: comma after exclamation/interjection
650.description: A comma immediately following an exclamatory word or emotional interjection (especially 'O'), marking a pause after an outburst of strong emotion

651.label: First-person pronoun starting new clause
651.description: The feature fires on 'I' when it begins a new independent clause immediately following a sentence boundary or clause break, often after a period or transition in dialogue

652.label: coordinating conjunction 'and' after comma
652.description: The feature fires on the word 'and' that follows a comma, functioning as a coordinating conjunction joining two clauses or phrases within a sentence

653.label: wit substring
653.description: Tokens containing the substring 'wit' or 'it' as part of words like 'withal', 'commit', 'conceit', 'remit', 'therewith', where the feature fires on the 'it' portion within these words

654.label: female name ending
654.description: Detects the final 'a' of feminine proper names (e.g., Helena, Cleopatra, Bianca, Katherina, Jessica, Angelica, Baptista Minola), typically Italian or classical feminine name endings

655.label: character name suffix
655.description: Firing on the final token of a Shakespeare character name (speaker label), particularly the ending portion of multi-token names like NORTHUMBERLAND, KATHERINE, PETRUCHIO, VINCENTIO, JOHN, SYRACUSE

656.label: contracted 't forms
656.description: Apostrophe-t contractions or elisions (e.g., ''t', ''twas', ''twere', ''tis', ''tame') where 't represents a shortened word like 'it', 'it was', 'it were', etc.

657.label: comma after coordinating conjunction at line/clause start
657.description: The feature fires on a comma that immediately follows 'And' or 'Or' at the beginning of a new clause or verse line, capturing the pattern of a conjunction followed directly by a comma in Shakespearean verse and prose.

658.label: em-dash interruption
658.description: The feature fires on em-dashes ('--') that mark interrupted, broken-off, or parenthetical speech mid-sentence, characteristic of Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue interruptions and asides

659.label: Contrastive conjunction after comma
659.description: The feature fires on 'but' or 'for' conjunctions that immediately follow a comma, introducing a contrastive or explanatory clause

660.label: sil/lo syllable onset
660.description: Fires on the token fragment 'sil' (as in silk, silence, silver) and occasionally 'lo' (as in lover's), capturing the onset syllable of specific words beginning with these letter sequences

661.label: word beginning with 't'
661.description: The feature fires on the letter 't' at the start of a word, particularly where that word follows a space or punctuation, capturing the onset of words beginning with the letter 't'

662.label: verb suffix mid-token
662.description: The feature fires on verb inflectional suffixes (-ive, -ite, -kes, -kes, -lt, -ens, -ves) that appear mid-token when the token boundary splits a verb form across subword tokens

663.label: KING speaker tag
663.description: The feature fires on the word 'KING' when it appears as a dramatic speaker label (character name prefix) in Shakespeare's plays, specifically for kings identified by title before their spoken lines.

664.label: abstract noun at line break
664.description: The feature activates on abstract or collective nouns (peace, rest, worst, redemption, destruction, everything, time, cause, voice) that appear near the end of a verse line or syntactic phrase, often preceded by a determiner or preposition

665.label: short affirmative/evaluative adjective after copula
665.description: Feature fires on short predicate adjectives (true, strange, sick) appearing after a form of 'to be' ('tis, is, am) in brief confirmatory or exclamatory clauses

666.label: Capitalized D word after punctuation
666.description: The feature fires on words beginning with capital letter 'D' that follow sentence-ending or clause-ending punctuation marks (period, exclamation mark, question mark), representing sentence-initial or speech-initial positions in Shakespearean text

667.label: possessive + soul/time
667.description: The feature fires on the words 'soul' or 'time' when preceded by a possessive or determiner (e.g., 'my soul', 'the time'), particularly in emotionally or dramatically charged contexts in Shakespeare

668.label: character name ending in -de or Pompey
668.description: Feature activates on specific character names: 'Conrade', 'Marcade', 'Cade', and 'Pompey' as speaker labels or references in Shakespeare dialogue

669.label: Exclamatory 'O'
669.description: The feature fires on the exclamatory interjection 'O' used as an emotional outcry or apostrophe, typically at the start of an impassioned speech or lament in Shakespearean dialogue

670.label: preposition 'at'
670.description: The feature fires on the word 'at' used as a preposition indicating location, direction, or point in time

671.label: or digraph mid-word
671.description: The feature activates on the letters 'or' appearing in the middle of a word, capturing this specific two-letter sequence regardless of the surrounding context.

672.label: rep/M prefix tokens
672.description: Fires on tokens that are the beginning fragment 'rep' or 'M' at the start of a word, particularly in words like 'replete', 'reprisal', 'replied', 'Mantua', 'Mars', 'Martius', 'Mercury', 'Mamillius'

673.label: 'Tis/'Twas opener
673.description: The feature detects the apostrophe character that begins contracted archaic forms 'Tis or 'Twas at the start of a clause or speech, typically following a speaker label or period

674.label: word following 'the'
674.description: The feature fires on a word that immediately follows the definite article 'the', particularly nouns like 'edge', 'verses', 'iron', 'ice', 'essay'

675.label: imperative/interrogative 'you' after verb
675.description: The feature fires on the word 'you' when it immediately follows a verb in either an imperative construction (get you, bear you, hold you, stand you) or a direct question directed at the interlocutor (Are you, Was't you, Why went you), capturing verb-'you' inversion or direct-address patterns common in Early Modern English

676.label: letter 'w' mid-token
676.description: The feature fires on the letter 'w' appearing within or at the start of a word, particularly where 'w' follows a prefix or is embedded mid-token (e.g., 'likewise', 'forsworn', 'underwrought', 'beware', 'therewith', 'dark-working')

677.label: "io" substring in name
677.description: The feature fires on the 'io' character sequence within Italian/classical proper names (Antioch, Antonio, Hortensio, Cambio, Petruchio, Lucent io), specifically the 'io' digraph typical of Italian-style names in Shakespeare

678.label: su- prefix
678.description: The feature fires on tokens beginning with the letters 'su', spanning words like 'suits', 'sugar', 'succeeding', 'succeed', 'suitor', and 'sugared'

679.label: speaker transition newline
679.description: Detects the whitespace or newline character between a speaker's line and the next speaker label in dramatic dialogue, marking turn boundaries in Shakespeare's plays

680.description: The feature detects the specific punctuation sequence period-dash-dash («.--»), which appears to mark a speaker transition or editorial stage direction boundary in Shakespeare's texts.

681.label: possessive pronoun 'his' in mid-clause
681.description: The feature fires on the word 'his' when used as a possessive pronoun referring to a male antecedent, typically appearing mid-clause after a verb or preposition

682.label: cons- prefix
