682.description: The feature activates on tokens beginning with 'cons' (as in 'consummation', 'consul', 'consulting', 'consider', 'consist', 'consorted', 'consideration'), detecting the lexical fragment 'cons' at the start of a word token

683.label: '-al' suffix mid-token
683.description: Fires on the 'al' portion of words ending in the '-al' suffix (liberal, personal, deal, walk, heal, dial, universal, poetical, formal, pastoral, signal), where the token is split mid-word

684.label: Capital D after punctuation
684.description: The feature fires on the letter 'D' when it appears as the first character of a word that begins after a sentence-ending or clause-ending punctuation mark (period, comma, exclamation mark, or question mark), capturing capitalized D-initial words at syntactic boundaries.

685.label: word following punctuation or sentence boundary
685.description: Feature activates on words that appear at the start of a new clause or sentence, often after a period or as a connective/transitional word (e.g., 'So', 'Soon') beginning a new syntactic unit, or on nouns immediately following a determiner in a mid-sentence phrase

686.label: indefinite article 'an'
686.description: The feature activates on the word 'an' (indefinite article) or the prefix 'an-' at the start of a word, appearing before vowel-initial words or as part of a word like 'antique' or 'animals'

687.label: Capitalized 'A' token
687.description: The feature fires on the token 'A' when it appears as a capital letter, whether as an article, part of a proper noun/name (Aliena, Amazon, Aufidius, Ambassadors, Alexandria, Aeneas, Abbot, Appennines), or standalone word, particularly at the start of a name or title following punctuation or mid-sentence capitalization

688.label: infinitive_to_verb
688.description: Feature activates on the space/token before a verb that follows 'to' in infinitive constructions, particularly after 'I' or similar pronouns (e.g., 'to «strike»', 'to «fashion»', 'to «thee»')

689.label: newline/line break
689.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or newline character immediately following a line-ending punctuation mark (comma, semicolon, dash, period) in poetic or verse text, marking the boundary between verse lines

690.label: Thou at sentence/clause start
690.description: The feature fires on the word 'Thou' when it appears at the beginning of a new sentence or major clause, typically following a period or other terminal punctuation, often in direct address or accusation

691.label: If + pronoun
691.description: The feature fires on the pronoun ('I' or 'it') immediately following the conjunction 'If' at the start of a conditional clause

692.label: rest/restr
692.description: The feature activates on the token fragment 'rest' (or 'ct'), appearing as part of words like 'rest', 'restraint', 'restricting', or embedded in other words, particularly the substring 'rest' in any context

693.label: word starting with vowel after 'the'
693.description: The feature fires on a space or newline immediately preceding a word that begins with a vowel (a, e, i, o, u), typically following the word 'the' or similar determiners, capturing the the+vowel-initial-word boundary

694.label: sentence-ending period
694.description: The feature fires on period punctuation marks that end complete sentences, particularly at clause or sentence boundaries in prose dialogue

695.label: relative pronoun 'who' in appositive/relative clause
695.description: The feature fires on the word 'who' when it introduces a relative or appositive clause that further identifies or describes a previously named person or noun phrase

696.label: possessive pronoun after preposition
696.description: The feature fires on 'your' or 'my' when preceded by a preposition (on, at, in, with), typically in informal or comedic speech contexts in Shakespeare

697.label: ra- words
697.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'ra' (rained, rare, rapt, ravished, rages, ravens, raged, rain, raised, raught)

698.label: substring 'nd'/'ll'/'nd' in middle of word
698.description: The feature fires on letter sequences 'nd', 'll', or similar consonant clusters appearing in the middle or end of a word, specifically on those exact character n-grams within tokens

699.label: contrastive conjunction mid-clause
699.description: The feature fires on conjunctions 'but' or 'like' that introduce a contrasting or comparative clause, typically following a positive or neutral assertion

700.label: speaker turn boundary
700.description: Activates on the newline or whitespace character immediately following a closing speech/dialogue line, marking the transition point between one character's dialogue ending and the next speaker label beginning in Shakespeare's dramatic texts

701.label: dis- prefix
701.description: The feature activates on the token 'dis' at the start of words with the Latin prefix 'dis-' (disease, disdain, disedged, dismal, dispose, dissembling, dismay, disinherited, disquiet)

702.label: mid-word token
702.description: Feature activates on tokens that are fragments/middle portions of words (not word-initial or complete tokens), representing subword tokenization splits within longer words

703.label: letter 'o' mid-word
703.description: The feature fires on the letter 'o' appearing within a word (not at word boundaries), particularly in the middle or later portion of a word

704.label: past participle adjective suffix
704.description: Words ending in '-led', '-ling', or '-less' used as participial or adjectival modifiers, particularly archaic or poetic past participle forms embedded within noun phrases

705.label: Sentence-initial capital M
705.description: The feature fires on uppercase letter 'M' that begins a new sentence or clause after a period/punctuation, typically mid-text

706.label: pronoun before negated/conditional verb
706.description: Feature fires on a space or word following a pronoun (thou, he, I, etc.) that precedes a negated or conditional verb construction

707.label: lust/stay
707.description: Tokens that are parts of the words 'lust' or 'stay' (including inflected forms like 'lusty', 'lustily', 'staying', 'stays'), appearing mid-token as the feature fires on the substring within these specific words

708.label: newline/speaker change token
708.description: Fires on the whitespace or empty token immediately following a period or end of speech that precedes a new speaker label in dramatic dialogue

709.label: word-internal 'ish/ed/teach/seen/upon' suffixes at token boundary
709.description: The feature fires on tokens that are suffixes or partial word endings (e.g., -ish, -ished, -aced, -ighted, -elted, -teach, -seen, -upon) occurring at a morpheme or subword boundary within a larger word or phrase, particularly verb past-tense and derivational suffixes split across tokens

710.label: Period ending short speech
710.description: Feature activates on period punctuation that ends a short declarative or exclamatory statement in dialogue, typically a complete thought of moderate brevity spoken by a character

711.label: gho- stem
711.description: The feature activates on the token 'ho' as part of the word 'ghost' or 'ghostly' (and occasionally 'who'), capturing the substring 'ho' within these specific words in Shakespearean text

712.label: hast thou
712.description: The feature detects the archaic second-person singular verb form 'hast', typically appearing in interrogative constructions like 'what hast thou' or 'where hast thou/been', used in Shakespearean dialogue.

713.label: '-ve' suffix or 'up/down' particle
713.description: The feature activates on the final syllable or particle of verbs ending in '-ve' (thrive, strive, crave, starve, shrive) or directional particles 'up'/'down' following a verb, capturing the terminal segment of these specific verb forms

714.label: Speaker label after dialogue
714.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or newline token immediately following a character name/speaker label in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue, specifically at the transition point between the speaker attribution and their spoken lines

715.label: Mid-sentence 'And' after clause boundary
715.description: The feature fires on the word 'And' when it begins a new clause or sentence continuation following a period, comma, or clause-ending punctuation, functioning as a coordinating conjunction at a syntactic boundary between clauses

716.label: conditional 'if'
716.description: The feature fires on the word 'if' (or 'If') when introducing a conditional clause in dialogue or speech

717.label: thy (second person possessive pronoun)
717.description: The feature fires specifically on the archaic second-person possessive pronoun 'thy' in Shakespearean dialogue

718.label: Suffolk
718.description: The feature detects the token fragment 'uff' as part of the name 'Suffolk', a major character/title in Shakespeare's historical plays

719.label: ill
719.description: The feature activates on the word 'ill' (or morphemes starting with 'ill-' such as 'ill-boding', 'ill-beseeming', 'illustrate', 'illly'), detecting occurrences of this lexical item in Shakespearean text

720.label: direct address with 'sir'
720.description: The feature fires on the word 'sir' when used as a direct form of address or vocative in dialogue, typically preceded by interjections, imperatives, or polite phrases (e.g., 'Go you, sir', 'I pray you, sir', 'Come, sir', 'Well, sir')

721.label: second person object 'you'
721.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'you' (or occasionally 'men') when it appears as the direct or indirect object of a verb, often following 'have at', 'had you', 'seek you', or similar verbal constructions targeting a second-person referent.

722.label: exclamatory 'What/Ha,' interjection comma
722.description: Comma following an exclamatory interjection word (primarily 'What' or 'Ha') used as a dramatic expression of surprise or indignation in dialogue

723.label: First-person future contraction
723.description: The feature detects the contraction 'll (will) attached to a first-person pronoun (I or We), typically expressing a declared intention or resolve by a character in dialogue

724.label: forg- prefix
724.description: Tokens beginning with 'forg' as part of words like forget, forgive, forego, forgot, forgone, forgo, forges

725.label: newline/line-break token
725.description: Fires on whitespace or newline tokens that represent line breaks between verse lines in Shakespeare's poetry

726.label: light
726.description: The word 'light' (or its inflected form 'lightly') appearing in Shakespearean contexts, often with metaphorical or punning senses (e.g., brightness, weightlessness, moral looseness)

727.label: letter g in proper nouns/polysyllabic words
727.description: The feature activates on the letter 'g' appearing mid-word, particularly within the proper noun 'Burgundy' and other polysyllabic words containing 'g' (e.g., Eglamour, Bridgenorth, prerogative, misgrafted), most commonly at the 'g' in 'Bur-g-undy'.

728.label: royal/collective 'our'
728.description: The word 'our' used in contexts of collective or royal first-person plural possession, particularly in formal, military, or monarchical speech acts in Shakespearean drama

729.label: pronoun 'it' as subject or object
729.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'it' in various syntactic roles (subject, object, complement), particularly when 'it' refers back to a previously mentioned noun or proposition

730.label: ho/ha syllable onset
730.description: Words beginning with the syllable 'ho' or 'ha', particularly hollow, holloed, hopes, haled, hailstones, hap, hallowed

731.label: comma after noun phrase
731.description: Feature fires on commas that follow proper names, titles, or noun phrases in appositive or list constructions, typically in formal/historical enumerations

732.label: ve digraph in middle of word
732.description: Fires on the 've' substring within words, particularly in words like 'survey', 'reveal', 'convey', 'revered', 'unvexed', where 've' appears as an internal digraph spanning a morpheme boundary or within a root

733.label: character speech prefix
733.description: Activates on the newline or whitespace token immediately preceding an all-caps character name (stage direction speaker label) in Shakespeare's dramatic texts

734.label: sentence-final punctuation after speech line
734.description: Fires on the period or question mark that ends a character's spoken line in dramatic dialogue, immediately before another character's name/speech begins

735.label: letter 'r' preceded by vowel in middle of word
735.description: The feature fires on the letter 'r' when it appears in the interior of a word, typically following a vowel, as in 'contrary', 'integrity', 'approval', 'flatterer'

736.label: abstract nouns
736.description: The feature fires on abstract nouns referring to acts, pledges, or subjects of discussion, specifically the words 'oath', 'action', 'matter', and 'way' used in abstract or formal contexts

737.label: th+consonant cluster
737.description: The feature activates on 'th' followed by a consonant (r, r, r, ck, rl, ck, rown, rust, reat, ree, ird, irsty), i.e. 'thr-', 'thr-', 'thl-', 'thr-' consonant clusters beginning with digraph 'th'

738.label: preposition 'in'
738.description: The feature fires on the word 'in' (or the 'in' morpheme at the start of words like 'insurrection', 'injuries') functioning as a preposition or prefix in various syntactic contexts

739.label: su- prefix
739.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'su' (part of words like 'sung', 'suggested', 'sucked', 'sue', 'suck', 'succeed', 'subscribe', 'supped', 'superfluous', 'succeeded')

740.label: concessive/restrictive 'but'
740.description: The word 'but' used in a concessive, restrictive, or limiting sense (meaning 'only', 'merely', 'except', or introducing a contrast/condition), as opposed to coordinative conjunction 'but'

741.label: my Lord [Name]
741.description: The feature fires on 'Lord' when preceded by 'my' and followed by a proper name or title, marking the formal direct address phrase 'my Lord [of X / Name]' in Shakespearean dialogue

742.label: let/make go
742.description: The word 'go' appearing in the context of releasing, departing, or movement phrases, especially 'let go' or imperative constructions involving physical release or departure

743.label: pronoun after 'tell'
743.description: The feature activates on pronouns (us, him) and occasionally other words that immediately follow the verb 'tell', capturing the syntactic pattern of 'tell + [pronoun/object]' in imperative or narrative constructions

744.label: conditional/subjunctive 'would'
744.description: The feature activates on the word 'would' used in conditional, subjunctive, or hypothetical constructions across Shakespeare's works

745.label: substring 'it' in word
745.description: The feature fires on the letters 'it' appearing as a substring within a word (e.g., 'fruit', 'charitable', 'illiterate', 'traitors', 'profit', 'positive', 'commit', 'institute', 'audit')

746.label: prepositional 'the'
746.description: The word 'the' appearing immediately after a preposition (at, in, by, with, upon, from, into)

747.label: third person plural possessive
747.description: The feature fires on 'their' (or 'your' in plural address) when used as a possessive determiner referring to a group/plural subject

748.label: no/nob-
748.description: The feature fires on the token 'no' (or the 'no' prefix in words like 'noble', 'nobles', 'nobleness', 'nobility') across various syntactic and thematic contexts in Shakespeare.

749.label: character name followed by newline/speech
749.description: Detects the newline or whitespace token immediately after a character's name/speaker label in a Shakespeare dramatic script, marking the transition point between the speaker tag and their dialogue

750.label: ven/en substring
750.description: Tokens containing the letter sequence 'en' or 'ven' embedded within a word, particularly in Middle/Early Modern English spellings like 'craven', 'seven', 'cloven', 'graveness', 'forgiven', 'length', 'lenity', 'burden'

751.label: contracted 'it'
751.description: The feature detects the apostrophe in contracted forms of 'it', specifically the elided 't or 'tis constructions common in Early Modern English (e.g., do't, let him know't, 'tis)

752.label: medial syllable or morpheme fragment
752.description: The feature activates on partial words or syllables that appear mid-token, specifically on internal phonetic/orthographic chunks within longer words (e.g., 'ury' in 'fury', 'ith' in 'pith', 'ine' in 'nine', 'line', 'ine'), as well as short function words or word fragments appearing after a space mid-phrase

753.label: Character label token
753.description: Fires on the word 'FIRST' or 'SECOND' when it appears as part of a speaker/character label (e.g. 'FIRST LORD', 'FIRST GENTLEMAN') in Shakespearean dramatic dialogue

754.label: end of verse line
754.description: Activates on the newline/whitespace token immediately following the end of a complete line of verse or rhyming poetry, typically at a line break between rhyming couplets or stanzas

755.label: h-word
755.description: The feature activates on words beginning with the letter 'h' (hides, honey, higher, host, hire, houses, hinges, hight, hips, hits, hive)

756.label: we- words
756.description: Feature fires on the 'we' portion of words beginning with 'we' (wealth, weapon/weapons, weary, weeds), capturing the bigram or subword token 'we' at the start of these specific vocabulary items

757.label: newline after question/exclamation
757.description: Feature fires on newline/whitespace characters that immediately follow a question mark or exclamation mark ending a line of dialogue

758.label: newline/scene break after speech
758.description: Activates on whitespace or line break tokens that immediately follow the end of a character's spoken line, typically at a speaker transition boundary in dramatic dialogue

759.label: substring 'im'
759.description: The feature activates on tokens containing the letter sequence 'im', including words like 'nimble', 'nimb', 'swim', 'trim', 'grim', 'aim', 'circum', 'animus', and 'imprimis'

760.label: is/is-like digraph
760.description: The feature fires on the letter sequence 'is' (or phonetically similar digraphs like 'il', 'as') appearing mid-word, most strongly on words containing 'fish'/'fis' but also 'friskins', 'lisping', 'treasury', 'filth'

761.label: function words mid-phrase
761.description: Short function words (prepositions, conjunctions, possessive pronouns: 'to', 'in', 'and', 'thy', 'my', 'such') appearing mid-clause within longer poetic or rhetorical constructions

762.label: stanza/section break
762.description: Closing quotation mark or line ending that marks a boundary between stanzas, couplets, or dramatic sections, typically appearing as whitespace or paragraph breaks between poetic units

763.label: auxiliary 'have' in perfect/conditional constructions
763.description: The feature fires on the word 'have' when used as an auxiliary verb in perfect tense or conditional/subjunctive constructions (e.g., 'I have done', 'should have', 'would have', 'I'd have')

764.label: courtly/social register nouns
764.description: Tokens that are parts of words or words relating to courtly life, social status, or formal social contexts (e.g., 'court', 'naughty' in archaic sense of worthless, 'courtesy', 'letter', 'friends', 'price')

765.label: ab- prefix continuation
765.description: The feature fires on the 'ab' token that begins words with the prefix 'ab-' (abused, abjured, abridged, aboard, abroad, abatement, ability), capturing the start of ab-prefixed words after tokenization splits them.

766.label: speaker change newline token
766.description: Fires on the whitespace/newline token immediately following a closing guillemet or punctuation that marks the end of one speaker's line and precedes a new speaker label in dramatic dialogue

767.label: multi-syllable word split at morpheme/syllable boundary
767.description: Feature activates on the second part of a word that has been split across a line or token boundary, particularly on internal syllables like 'ce' in 'decease', 'once', 'means', 'worth', 'return', 'force', 'heart' — i.e., tokens that are mid-word continuations following a break

768.label: word boundary space
768.description: The feature fires on a space character that follows a word and precedes the continuation of a sentence, typically at a mid-clause boundary after a syntactically significant word or phrase

769.label: line break after period/sentence end
769.description: Feature activates on whitespace or newline immediately following a sentence-ending period, marking the boundary between two sentences or verse lines

770.label: letter 'n' after article/preposition 'a'
770.description: The feature fires on the letter 'n' at the start of a word that follows the indefinite article 'a', capturing the 'a n-' bigram boundary

771.label: sentence-ending period
771.description: The feature fires on period punctuation marks that end complete sentences, particularly in dialogue or prose contexts within Shakespeare's works

772.label: young
772.description: The feature fires on the word 'young' used as an adjective or standalone descriptor, typically referring to a person's age or youthfulness in Shakespearean dialogue

773.label: partial word ending in 'ors'/'ers'/'ors'
773.description: Tokens that are the suffix fragment '-ors', '-ers', or '-ors' completing words like 'errors', 'doors', 'terrors', 'creditors', 'doctors', 'horses', or whole words like 'friends', 'others'

774.label: character name truncation
774.description: Feature fires on the middle portion of split/truncated character names (stage directions or speaker labels) such as CHAMBERLA[IN], GUILD[EN]STERN, DOL[A]BELLA, where the token falls within a multi-token proper name

775.label: Go to
775.description: The phrase 'Go to' used as an Elizabethan/Shakespearean dismissive exclamation or interjection, where the feature fires on the word 'to' following 'Go'

776.label: coordinating conjunction 'And'/'and'/'would' at clause boundary
776.description: The feature fires on conjunctions (primarily 'And'/'and', occasionally 'would') that appear at the start of a new clause or sentence, often after a line break or major syntactic boundary, linking two independent or semi-independent clauses in Shakespearean verse or prose

777.label: infinitive 'to'
777.description: The word 'to' functioning as an infinitive marker introducing a verb phrase

778.label: character name suffix
778.description: The feature fires on the final letters of the character name 'HORTENSIO' (and occasionally similar names like 'HENRY'), specifically on the 'IO'/'O' ending that completes a speaking character's stage direction label

779.label: into
779.description: The word 'into' indicating movement, transition, or entry into a state/place

780.label: pronoun after reporting/epistemic verb
780.description: Feature fires on pronouns (I, you, he, that) immediately following verbs of saying, thinking, or knowing (say, think, know, fed)

781.label: second person possessive 'your'
781.description: The feature activates on the word 'your' (second person possessive pronoun) in Shakespearean text, particularly in contexts of direct address, commands, or accusations directed at a person or group

782.label: letter 'n' in specific word contexts
782.description: The feature fires on the letter 'n' within words containing 'mn', 'rn', 'ln', 'n+vowel' patterns, particularly in words like 'solemn', 'darnel', 'calumnious', 'hymen', 'pattern', 'differences', 'prunes' — specifically the 'n' character in polysyllabic words with nasal consonant clusters or Latin-derived suffixes

783.label: my [title/name]
783.description: The word 'my' used as a respectful address preceding a title or name (e.g., 'my lord', 'my lady', 'my liege'), typically in response or greeting contexts in Shakespearean dialogue

784.label: pl token
784.description: Fires on the token fragment 'pl' as part of a word beginning with 'pl' (plot, place, plume, plainsong, pledge, planets, planks, plows, plum, places)

785.label: the word 'here'
785.description: The feature activates specifically on the word 'here' (including 'herein'), used in various syntactic positions (adverbial, locative, temporal) within Shakespearean dialogue

786.label: ic digraph
786.description: The feature activates on the letter sequence 'ic' within words, particularly in the middle of words like 'officer', 'relic', 'sufficient', 'delicate', 'chronicle', etc.

787.label: "one" as indefinite pronoun/numeral
787.description: The feature activates on the word 'one' used as an indefinite pronoun or numeral in various syntactic contexts

788.label: Proper noun mid-token split
788.description: The feature fires on the second syllable or internal segment of a proper noun (Julius, Juliet, Julia, Capitol, Mars) at a mid-word token boundary

789.label: k/sk digraph
789.description: Tokens beginning with the letter 'k' (or 'sk') immediately following a space or word boundary, particularly in mid-sentence positions

790.label: imperative make
790.description: The feature detects the word 'make' used in an imperative or optative construction, where a character is commanding, urging, or wishing that something be done

791.label: comma after list item or conjunction
791.description: Feature fires on commas that follow items in a list or coordinating conjunctions (especially 'and', 'you') in enumerative or parallel syntactic structures

792.label: speaker label after line break
792.description: The feature fires on the whitespace/newline token immediately preceding an all-caps character name (stage direction speaker label) in dramatic dialogue

793.label: as [pronoun]
793.description: The feature fires on a pronoun (it/you/thou) immediately following the comparative/equative conjunction 'as', in constructions like 'as it is', 'as you please', 'as soon as you'

794.label: le- word start
794.description: The feature activates on the token 'le' as the beginning of a word (e.g., leisure, legs, lean, level, legitimate, legate, levy), capturing the 'le' prefix/onset across diverse lexical items

795.label: GLOUCESTER
795.description: The feature activates on the character name 'GLOUCESTER' as a speaker label/stage direction token in Shakespeare's plays

796.label: second person verb forms
796.description: Tokens that complete second-person verb constructions (you said/believe/deserved/know/guess/profess/etc.), specifically the ending portions of verbs following 'you'

797.label: discourse connective 'Then'
797.description: The feature activates on the word 'Then' (or occasionally other words) used as a logical or narrative connective at the start of a clause or speech, signaling a consequential or sequential inference (e.g., 'Then must I think...', 'Then let's make haste...', 'Then was your sin...')

798.label: demonstrative 'those'
798.description: The feature fires on the demonstrative pronoun/determiner 'those', regardless of syntactic role, position, or thematic context.

799.label: word ending in -es
799.description: Tokens that are the suffix '-es' completing a word, typically plural nouns or third-person singular verb forms ending in '-es'

800.label: '-al' or '-er' suffix syllable
800.description: The feature fires on the second syllable of words ending in '-al' or '-er' (e.g., 'metal', 'silver', 'lover', 'liberal', 'prodigal'), specifically on the final syllable token of these suffix patterns

801.label: st token
801.description: The feature fires on the token 'st' as part of a larger word, specifically the digraph 'st' appearing mid-word or at word boundaries in Shakespearean text

802.label: preposition 'with' after verb/phrase
802.description: The feature fires on the word 'with' when it appears as a preposition following a verb or verbal phrase, typically introducing an accompaniment, instrument, or manner complement

803.label: ll digraph
803.description: The feature activates on the second 'l' of a double-l ('ll') sequence within words

804.label: let me
804.description: The feature fires on the word 'me' when immediately preceded by 'let', detecting the fixed two-word phrase 'let me' used as a request or plea by a speaker

805.label: plural nouns ending in -s
805.description: Tokens that are plural noun endings (-ses, -ses, -ers, -owers, etc.) appearing mid-word or as word-final plural morphemes on nouns

806.label: mid-word boundary after prefix
806.description: Feature fires on letters that immediately follow a common English prefix (im-, pain-, mess-, any-, W-), capturing the morpheme boundary or stem-initial character within a word

807.label: word-final 'es' suffix
807.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the 'es' ending of a word, typically forming plural nouns or third-person singular verb forms (e.g., 'increases', 'resumes', 'includes', 'crosses', 'shames', 'prerogatives', 'substitutes', 'tales', 'purposes', 'ones')

808.label: comma in enumeration/list
808.description: Comma separating items in a list or enumeration of nouns, adjectives, or phrases

809.label: word starting with 'l'
809.description: The feature fires on the letter 'l' at the beginning of a word, particularly words like 'length', 'language', 'large', 'line', 'lute', 'latch', 'lascivious', 'lace'

810.label: the following noun phrase
810.description: Feature fires on the word 'the' when it precedes a noun phrase that introduces a new topic, target, or point of focus, often after a discourse pivot or speaker turn

811.label: letter 'w'
811.description: The feature activates on the letter 'w' at the start of a word, particularly in contexts where the word beginning with 'w' follows a space or punctuation

812.label: mid-word line break or split token
812.description: Feature activates on tokens that are split mid-word or occur at unusual word/line boundaries, including broken words like 'ra' in 'dispraised/thralled', truncated speaker labels like 'PLEBEIAN', and empty space tokens following short complete utterances

813.label: Character address opener
813.description: The word 'My' at the start of a character's speech turn, typically following a stage direction or speaker label, used as a respectful or affectionate address opener

814.label: I'll / We'll / They'll contraction
814.description: The feature fires on the contraction 'll (will) attached to a pronoun (I, we, they), typically expressing a first or third person future commitment or promise

815.label: speak/talk/part verbs
815.description: The feature activates on verb forms of 'speak', 'talk', and 'part' (including conjugations and past participles), particularly at the token boundary of these communication or separation verbs

816.label: coordinating conjunction 'and' after semicolon or clause boundary
816.description: The feature fires on the word 'and' when it appears immediately after a semicolon or at a major clause boundary, functioning as a coordinating conjunction joining two independent or semi-independent clauses

817.label: simile marker 'like'
817.description: The word 'like' used as a simile/comparison connector, introducing figurative comparisons in poetic or dramatic prose

818.label: em-dash token
818.description: The feature fires on the '--' (em-dash or double-hyphen) punctuation token used as a dash in Shakespeare's text

819.label: possessive pronoun + noun of status/address
819.description: Feature fires on nouns that follow a possessive pronoun (Your, my, his) and denote a title, role, or honorific (Grace, voice, state, wife, England, York)

820.label: sentence-ending period after 'you'
820.description: The feature fires on a period that ends a sentence where the last word before the period is 'you'

821.label: French text letter 'e' or 'o'
821.description: Fires on the letters 'e' and 'o' appearing within French-language dialogue, particularly vowels in French words in Shakespeare's Henry V language lesson scene

822.label: newline after sentence-ending punctuation
822.description: Feature fires on whitespace/newline tokens that immediately follow a period or sentence-final punctuation mark, typically at the end of a complete utterance in dramatic dialogue

823.label: make/makes them
823.description: The phrase 'make them' or 'makes them' where the feature fires on the word 'them' immediately following 'make' or 'makes'

824.label: possessive pronoun in third/first person context
824.description: The feature activates on possessive determiners (his, my, our) and occasionally attributive adjectives appearing mid-clause, typically preceding a noun in a descriptive or enumerative prose passage

825.label: sworn/forsworn vow context
825.description: Tokens appearing in contexts involving oaths, vows, swearing, or forswearing, particularly around themes of love oaths and their consequences in Shakespeare

826.label: des- prefix words
826.description: Words beginning with the syllable 'des' (design, desert, descend, desolation, desperate, descried, desires, deserts)

827.label: past tense 'did'
827.description: The feature fires on the word 'did' used as a past tense auxiliary or main verb in declarative clauses, particularly in third-person singular constructions like 'he did' or 'that he did'

828.label: character name abbreviation
828.description: Fires on the middle letters of abbreviated Shakespeare character names in stage directions/dialogue labels (e.g., PUCELLE→PUC«EL»LE, CYMBELINE→CYM«BEL»INE, JEWELER→JEW«EL»ER, STEPHANO→ST«EPH»ANO)

829.label: imperative interjection
829.description: Short imperative or exclamatory words used as commands or emphatic interjections (e.g., 'stand', 'look', 'even') typically appearing after a pause or scene direction

830.label: multi-syllable word middle
830.description: Feature fires on middle syllables of polysyllabic words, specifically the non-initial, non-final interior portions of longer words split across subword tokens

831.label: newline/line-break token
831.description: The feature fires on the newline or line-break token (represented as » «) that separates verse lines in Shakespeare's works, marking the boundary between poetic lines

832.label: preposition/verb after noun phrase
832.description: Feature activates on prepositions ('upon', 'unto') or short verbs ('show', 'make') that follow a longer noun or object phrase, typically mid-clause

833.label: question mark closing short question
833.description: The feature fires on '?' tokens that close brief, often single-clause rhetorical or direct questions spoken in dialogue, typically 2–6 words long

834.label: y after vowel cluster
834.description: The feature fires on the letter 'y' when it appears as the final character of words containing 'ey', 'oy', 'ay', or similar vowel+y sequences, particularly in words like 'journey', 'attorney', 'rosy', 'giddy', 'key', 'moiety', 'flattery', 'heresy'

835.label: line break after period/comma
835.description: Detects the newline or line boundary token (blank space representing a verse line break) that follows punctuation at the end of a poetic line in Shakespeare's verse, marking the transition between successive lines of poetry

836.label: whence/thence
836.description: The token 'ce' completing the archaic adverbs 'whence' or 'thence', meaning 'from which place' or 'from that place'

837.label: Affirmative 'Ay' response
837.description: The feature detects the word 'Ay' used as an affirmative response or agreement by a character in dialogue, typically appearing at the start of a spoken reply in Shakespearean dramatic text.

838.label: '-ent' suffix tokens
838.description: Tokens that are the final syllable or partial token of words ending in the suffix '-ent' (e.g., agent, impediment, impertinent, impatient, improvident, parliament, present, confident, magnificent, absent)

839.label: y/j letter onset
839.description: The feature fires on tokens beginning with the letters 'y' or 'j', capturing words like 'yard', 'yellow', 'yew', 'yesterday', 'yeoman', 'jurisdiction', 'jaw'

840.label: ste/u digraph
840.description: Tokens that are the letters 'ste' or 'u' appearing as part of words containing the 'stu' or 'ste' sequence, particularly in words like 'steel', 'steeds', and 'dukes/duly' where the digraph represents a stressed vowel or consonant cluster

841.label: letter n in specific contexts
841.description: The feature fires on the letter 'n' when it appears as part of words containing the sequence 'rn' or 'nn' or 'mn', particularly in words like 'worn', 'external', 'outrun', 'ornament', 'pattern', 'damnation', 'dinner'

842.label: coordinating conjunction 'and'
842.description: The feature fires on the word 'and' used as a coordinating conjunction linking two adjectives, phrases, or clauses

843.label: Character name label
843.description: The feature fires on uppercase character name tokens (speaker labels) that appear as dialogue attribution markers in Shakespeare's dramatic texts, particularly 'OTHELLO' and similar character cues preceding spoken lines

844.label: song/verse line start
844.description: Token position at the beginning of a sung verse, song lyric, or rhyming poetic line within a play, often following a speaker cue or preceding sung stanzas

845.label: prepositional 'at/in/to the/a'
845.description: Feature fires on the article 'the' or 'a' immediately following a preposition (at, in, to, upon), marking the transition from preposition to noun phrase

846.label: Capitalized M after special token/boundary
846.description: The feature fires on uppercase letter 'M' that appears at the start of a word following a dialogue speaker tag, stage direction, or sentence boundary — particularly names/titles like 'Madam', 'Messenger', 'Most', 'Mine', 'Made', 'Methinks', 'Monday' beginning a new speech or clause

847.label: word starting with 'd'
847.description: The feature fires on tokens that are the letter 'd' beginning a word, particularly verb forms starting with 'd' (dies, danced, determined, dig, dote, dallied, dash, dine)

848.label: plural possessive or plural 's'
848.description: The feature activates on the letter 's' that forms a plural or possessive suffix on nouns (e.g., 'villains', 'towns', 'fields', 'maids', 'twigs', 'walls', 'devils')

849.label: adjective with comparative/general meaning
849.description: Tokens that are adjectives or adjective-like modifiers expressing extent, generality, or reciprocity (e.g., 'another', 'other', 'general', 'further', 'warlike', 'angry'), often appearing mid-clause modifying a noun

850.label: -ness suffix
850.description: Tokens that are the '-ness' suffix completing abstract nouns (boldness, greatness, sickness, tenderness, goodness, briefness, hardness)

851.label: Capital W after punctuation
851.description: The feature fires on the letter 'W' at the start of a new clause or sentence following closing punctuation or a line break, capturing sentence-initial or clause-initial capital W tokens in dramatic dialogue

852.label: plural suffix '-ers'/'-ings'
852.description: The feature activates on the letters 'ers' or 'ings' that form the plural or gerund/plural suffix at the end of words, specifically when these characters are tokenized as a separate token following the word stem

853.label: word-internal suffix position
853.description: Feature activates on letters that are part of a suffix or word ending, specifically mid-to-late characters within a word that spans a line break or token boundary, often adverbial/participial suffixes like -ly, -ing, -ance, -er

854.label: article 'the' after sentence boundary
854.description: The feature fires on the word 'the' when it appears at or near the start of a new sentence or clause, typically following a period, question mark, or other sentence-ending punctuation within dialogue

855.label: indefinite article 'a' after semicolon or list
855.description: The feature fires on the indefinite article 'a' when it appears after a semicolon, comma-separated list item, or similar syntactic boundary, often introducing a new noun phrase in an enumerative or contrastive context

856.label: infinitive 'To' after predicate
856.description: The feature fires on the word 'To' when it begins an infinitive phrase that serves as the purpose, result, or complement of a preceding clause or predicate expression

857.label: discourse connective at clause boundary
857.description: The feature fires on 'Therefore' or 'Now' when used as a logical/discourse connective at the start of a new clause or sentence, signaling a conclusion or consequence drawn from preceding reasoning

858.label: preposition following feminine pronoun context
858.description: Short prepositions ('of', 'with', 'into', 'unto', 'from') appearing in contexts describing a female character's actions, relationships, or attributes

859.label: coordinating conjunction
859.description: Feature fires on 'and' or 'not' connecting two parallel noun phrases, adjective phrases, or clauses, typically mid-sentence between semantically paired or contrasting elements

860.label: preposition 'to'
860.description: The feature fires on the word 'to' used as a preposition or infinitive marker in directional, purposive, or recipient contexts

861.label: speaker change after punctuation
861.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or empty token immediately following a sentence-ending punctuation mark (period, exclamation mark, question mark) that precedes a new speaker label in dramatic dialogue

862.label: complementizer 'That'
862.description: The word 'That' functioning as a subordinating conjunction introducing a complement or relative clause, typically following a main clause expressing emotion, assertion, or description

863.label: ou digraph
863.description: The feature detects the letters 'ou' appearing together within a word, firing on the 'ou' portion of words like 'touched', 'touches', 'poured', etc.

864.label: Capitalized E at turn/line start
864.description: The feature fires on a capitalized letter 'E' that begins a new speech or clause, typically following a speaker attribution or sentence boundary, representing the first letter of words like 'Eros', 'Even', 'E'en', 'Esperance', 'Everyone', 'Ely'

865.label: end of speech newline
865.description: Whitespace or newline token immediately following the closing of a character's speech, marking the boundary between one speaker's dialogue and the next stage direction or speaker label

866.label: short interrogative clause
866.description: Feature activates on tokens that appear within or immediately following a short question or interrogative phrase in dialogue

867.label: I (first person pronoun) after speaker tag
867.description: The feature fires on the word 'I' when it appears as the first word of a character's spoken line, immediately following a speaker attribution or stage direction

868.label: first-person 'I' as subject after clause boundary
868.description: The feature fires on the token 'I' when it appears as a first-person subject pronoun immediately following a clause or sentence boundary (conjunction, period, or similar syntactic break), often introducing a new independent clause spoken by a character.

869.label: sentence-ending punctuation
869.description: Feature fires on period or exclamation mark characters that end a complete sentence or clause, typically at syntactic boundaries between statements

870.label: St capital or mid-word
870.description: Token is 'st' or 'St' appearing either as a capitalized word-initial digraph (line-initial or after punctuation) or embedded within a word, representing the letters S followed by t

871.label: pe/let syllable fragment
871.description: The feature fires on the substring 'pe' or 'let' appearing mid-token, particularly as parts of words like 'open', 'stupefy', 'ripe', 'wipe', or the name 'Hamlet'

872.label: word-initial 'h'
872.description: The feature fires on the letter 'h' at the start of a word, capturing the initial 'h' character across various words (horns, honor, honey, house, honors, horse, husband, hours, humor, hurts, hundred)

873.label: their
873.description: The feature detects the word 'their' (or 'theirs'), firing specifically on this third-person plural possessive pronoun

874.label: word starting with 'e' after space
874.description: The feature fires on the letter 'e' that begins a word preceded by a space, capturing the boundary between a space and an 'e'-initial word (e.g., 'ease', 'eloquence', 'epitaph', 'events', 'evidence', 'eel-skins', 'elbow-room', 'effigies')

875.label: cur- prefix token
875.description: The feature activates on the token 'cur' (or similar short tokens like 'ac', 'ong') that appear as the first part of a split word containing the letters 'cur' or similar subword fragments, predominantly the prefix 'cur' in words like 'currents', 'curbs', 'curls', 'cursed', 'curtsy'

876.label: must
876.description: The word 'must' (or its morphological variants) appearing in contexts of obligation, necessity, or compulsion, often in political or authoritative statements

877.label: First-person pronoun 'I' starting a new clause after punctuation
877.description: The feature activates on the word 'I' when it begins a new independent clause or sentence, typically following a period, comma, or other boundary marker mid-text

878.label: vocative or clause boundary comma
878.description: Fires on commas that follow a clause or phrase boundary, particularly after vocatives, conjunctions, or short clauses that introduce a new clause or direct address

879.label: first-person possessive pronoun
879.description: The feature fires on the word 'my' used as a possessive pronoun by a speaking character, typically mid-clause referring to something belonging to the narrator/speaker

880.label: sl- prefix
880.description: Token begins with the letters 'sl', capturing the fragment 'sl' at the start of words like 'slander', 'slut', 'slow', 'slaughter', 'slight', 'slash', etc.

881.label: line break in verse
881.description: Newline or line boundary character within poetic/song verse passages in Shakespeare

882.label: disjunctive 'or'
882.description: The feature activates on the word 'or' (and once 'oracle' where 'or' begins the token) used as a disjunctive conjunction presenting alternatives, choices, or paired options within a clause

883.label: copula 'is' as complete/near-complete predicate
883.description: The feature fires on the word 'is' when it functions as the entire or primary predicate in a very short affirmative or confirmatory clause (e.g., 'It is', 'He is', 'She is'), often as a standalone confirmation or brief assertion

884.label: FALSTAFF speaker tag
884.description: Fires on the token 'FALSTAFF' when it appears as a speaker/character label introducing dialogue in Shakespeare's plays

885.label: semicolon
885.description: Token is a semicolon used as a clause separator in Shakespeare's prose or verse

886.label: ad/ade substring in word
886.description: The feature fires on the letters 'ad' or 'ade' appearing within words, including 'tread', 'load', 'plead', 'invades', 'contradiction', 'shade', etc.

887.label: French language text
887.description: Tokens appearing within French dialogue, particularly in the French lesson scene from Henry V featuring Katherine and Alice

888.label: word-final '-ity'/'-age'/'-ief'/'-ue'/'-ars' suffixes
888.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the final syllable or morpheme of a multisyllabic word, particularly word-internal or word-final suffixes such as '-ity', '-age', '-ears', '-ief', '-ue', often at the end of a stressed rhyming or metrically significant word in verse

889.label: sentence-ending period
889.description: Period punctuation mark that ends a complete sentence or utterance, typically in dialogue

890.label: third-person singular present tense suffix
890.description: Tokens containing the 'es' ending of third-person singular present tense verbs (e.g., pleases, confesses, accuses, crosses, notes, tastes, escapes)

891.label: interrogative 'what'
891.description: The feature fires on the word 'what' used as an interrogative or relative pronoun introducing an embedded question or noun clause

892.label: Well as discourse marker
892.description: The token 'Well' (or compound starting with 'Well') used as a conversational/discourse marker at the start of a speech or clause, signaling a response, transition, or mild concession in dialogue

893.label: bre/ore substring
893.description: Tokens containing the letter sequence 'bre' or 'ore' (parts of words like 'bred', 'bread', 'breeds', 'bore')

894.label: 'Tis
894.description: The feature detects the archaic contraction ''Tis' (it is), specifically when it appears at the start of a speech or clause following a speaker attribution or sentence boundary in Shakespearean dialogue

895.label: end of speech/line followed by newline
895.description: Fires on the whitespace or newline character immediately after a closing quotation mark, period, or speech-ending punctuation that concludes a character's dialogue turn, just before the next speaker label begins

896.label: em-dash interruption
896.description: The feature fires on '--' tokens that function as em-dashes marking parenthetical interruptions, asides, or syntactic breaks within a sentence

897.label: character speech label
897.description: The feature fires on the newline/space token immediately following a character name tag (e.g. QUEEN MARGARET, KING RICHARD, DUCHESS) that introduces a new line of dialogue, detecting the positional boundary between a speaker label and the start of their spoken lines.

898.label: imperative 'Come' as scene transition
898.description: The feature fires on the word 'Come' used as an imperative/command to direct characters to move or follow, typically at a scene transition or shift in action, often preceded by a dash or period

899.label: farewell utterance
899.description: The feature detects the word 'farewell' used as a parting utterance or exclamation in dialogue, typically at a moment of departure or dismissal

900.label: relative clause pivot
900.description: Fires on the whitespace/newline token immediately following a relative pronoun (which, who, whom, that) or equivalent connector that introduces a subordinate clause

901.label: the + noun phrase boundary
901.description: Fires on the space token immediately following the word 'the' when 'the' precedes a significant noun or noun phrase, particularly in formal or literary prose contexts

902.label: al substring
902.description: The feature detects the letter sequence 'al' appearing within words, typically as part of suffixes (-eal, -ial, -al, -alm) or internal syllables containing 'al'

903.label: blank/empty line
903.description: Whitespace or empty line breaks between lines of verse or dialogue in Shakespeare's plays

904.label: 'it' as subject after location/relative clause
904.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'it' when it appears immediately after a relative or locative clause (introduced by 'where', 'whereon', 'which', 'that', etc.), functioning as the grammatical subject of the following clause

905.label: silent 'e' in middle of word
905.description: The feature activates on the letter 'e' that appears mid-token in words where the 'e' is part of a digraph, archaic spelling, or silent/unstressed position (e.g., 'shoe', 'borne', 'frieze', 'soever', 'breeched', 'grievous', 'profane')

906.label: ANTONY speaker label
906.description: The feature fires on the character name 'ANTONY' (and once 'PRINCE EDWARD') when it appears as a dramatic speaker label/stage direction introducing dialogue in Shakespeare's plays

907.label: sc- token
907.description: The feature fires on the token 'sc' when a word beginning with 'sc' is split across tokens, capturing the 'sc' prefix of words like 'scruples', 'scene', 'scurvy', 'scroyles', 'scruple', 'scroll', 'scarecrow', 'screech', 'scabbard', 'scoffer', 'scape', 'scourge'

908.label: 'thou' followed by comma or punctuation
908.description: The feature fires on punctuation marks (primarily commas) that immediately follow the word 'thou' or related second-person pronoun forms, marking a syntactic pause after direct address or subject pronoun in archaic English

909.label: hyphenated compound word
909.description: The feature activates on hyphens within compound words or hyphenated expressions (e.g., 'well-steeled', 'spell-stopped', 'soon-speeding', 'free-footed', 'six-and-twenty')

910.label: speaker label
910.description: Activates on the whitespace/newline immediately following a character name speaker label in Shakespeare dramatic dialogue

911.label: interrogative auxiliary opener
911.description: Feature fires on auxiliary verbs (Will, Did, Can) that begin a direct question addressed to another character, typically mid-dialogue

912.label: comp- prefix split
912.description: The feature activates on the token 'comp' when it appears as the beginning of a word that has been split mid-token, specifically at the 'comp' fragment preceding letters like 'rehend', 'anion', 'are', 'assed', 'liment', 'osed', 'assion', 'ound', 'elled'

913.label: mid-word syllable in polysyllabic word
913.description: Feature activates on a middle or non-initial syllable fragment within a longer word, where the token is an internal chunk (e.g., 'et', 'umph', 'ance', 'ice', 'uce', 'el') of a word split across tokens

914.label: Character name stage directions
914.description: Detects character name labels (speaker tags) in Shakespeare's dramatic texts, specifically names like ISABELLA, DUKE, COUNTESS appearing as dialogue attribution markers

915.label: sentence-final punctuation followed by newline/speaker change
915.description: Fires on the whitespace or newline token immediately following a period or sentence-ending punctuation at a speaker turn boundary in dramatic dialogue

916.label: definite article after sentence boundary
916.description: The feature fires on 'the' (capitalized or lowercase) that appears at or near the start of a new clause or sentence, often following punctuation or a speaker attribution, introducing a noun phrase

917.label: of [Title/Name]
917.description: The word 'of' appearing in noble titles or honorifics, specifically in constructions like 'Lord of X', 'Earl of X', 'Duke of X', connecting a title to a place or name

918.label: pro- prefix
918.description: Tokens beginning with the substring 'pro' (as a prefix of a longer word such as 'proves', 'provokes', 'proper', 'producing', 'prodigal', 'proclaim', 'proceeds', 'probable')

919.label: acc- prefix
919.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'acc' (as in account, accusation, acclamation, accident, etc.)

920.label: Temporal/conditional clause opener after punctuation
920.description: The feature fires on 'When' or 'H' (starting 'Hark', 'Hath', 'Hear', 'Hie') that begins a new clause or sentence immediately following a punctuation mark (period, colon, comma, or quotation mark), functioning as a clause-initial capital letter after a boundary

921.label: capital L after punctuation/space
921.description: The feature fires on the letter 'L' when it begins a proper noun (name or place) that starts with capital L, particularly character names like Lepidus, Laertes, Leonato, Lancaster, Leonatus, or London

922.label: word starting with 'p'
922.description: The feature fires on the letter 'p' at the start of a word, specifically detecting the initial 'p' character of various words (philosophy, paltry, piping, pestilence, pillars, palm, pressed, picture, pagan, pestilent, petard)

923.label: syllable onset 'to'/'ra'/'for'
923.description: Feature fires on the first letters of a syllable or word where a line or token break splits a word like 'top', 'tooth', 'raven', 'ravishing', 'ford', capturing mid-word token boundaries where the continuation begins with specific phonetic patterns

924.label: conjunction/comma after 'you'
924.description: The feature fires on 'and' or ',' that immediately follows a word referencing the addressee (e.g., 'you', 'your servant', a name/title), functioning as a syntactic boundary after a second-person reference in dialogue

925.label: end of subordinate/relative clause
925.description: Feature fires on the space or newline token immediately following the end of a long noun phrase, subordinate clause, or relative clause, typically before the main clause resumes or a new syntactic unit begins

926.label: definite article 'the' before noun phrase
926.description: The feature fires on the word 'the' (definite article) appearing in dramatic dialogue, typically preceding a significant noun phrase in a command, claim, or action context within Shakespeare's history plays

927.label: question mark at end of spoken line
927.description: The feature activates on '?' characters that close a rhetorical or direct question spoken by a character in dialogue, typically mid-scene at the end of a verse or prose line

928.label: Speaker label / character name at start of dialogue
928.description: Fires on tokens that are part of a character name tag introducing a new speaker's line in dramatic dialogue, often at the beginning of a speech or after a stage direction

929.label: his + noun phrase
929.description: The feature fires on the possessive pronoun 'his' when it precedes a noun phrase describing a body part, physical location, or personal domain (e.g., chamber, shroud, limbs, bones, gate)

930.label: Speaker label after dialogue
930.description: Feature fires on the newline/space token immediately following a speaker label (e.g. FIRST LORD, SECOND LORD, DUKE) that introduces a new line of dialogue

931.label: coordinating/subordinating conjunction after clause
931.description: Fires on conjunctions (and, for, but, or) that connect clauses, appearing mid-sentence after a complete or near-complete clause unit

932.label: period ending quoted/narrative line
932.description: A period ('.' wrapped in guillemets) that closes a line of verse or prose, typically at the end of a complete syntactic unit such as a rhyming couplet line, narrative sentence, or speech line in Shakespearean text

933.label: sentence-ending punctuation
933.description: Fires on period or exclamation mark that ends a complete sentence, typically mid-dialogue before a speaker transition or continuation

934.label: feminine pronoun 'her'
934.description: The feature fires on the word 'her' used as a possessive determiner or object pronoun referring to a female character

935.label: object pronoun 'thee'
935.description: The feature fires on the word 'thee' used as a direct or indirect object (or object of a preposition) in Early Modern English dialogue, typically following a verb

936.label: speaker label after line break
936.description: The feature fires on the whitespace or newline token immediately preceding a new character's name/speaker label in Shakespeare dialogue, marking the transition between speeches

937.label: bl- token fragment
937.description: The feature fires on the token fragment 'bl' at the start of words beginning with 'bl' (blows, blown, blush, blurted, blasts, blossoms, blest, blench, blows, blot, blows)

938.label: sour/source
938.description: The feature activates on the substring 'our' within the words 'sour' and 'source', capturing this specific orthographic sequence in those two lexically related words

939.label: tha/shun digraph
939.description: Tokens containing the letter sequence 'th' or 'sh' followed by a vowel, specifically the subword fragments 'tha' and 'shun' (and variants)

940.label: newline/line break token
940.description: The feature fires on whitespace or newline tokens that represent a line or sentence boundary, typically appearing mid-passage where a new clause or sentence begins

941.label: sal/per/chan substring
941.description: Tokens containing the letter sequence 'sal', 'per', or 'chan' mid-word, particularly fragments of words like 'salute', 'salve', 'salutation', 'perceiv', 'perchance', 'mischance'

942.label: the word 'another'
942.description: The feature activates specifically on the token 'another' across various syntactic and thematic contexts in Shakespeare's works

943.label: short_response_opener
943.description: Feature activates on 'It' at the start of a short reply or response utterance by a character, typically following another character's statement or question, where 'It' begins a brief declarative or reactive sentence.

944.label: is/dis/vis syllable
944.description: Fires on the letters 'is' as part of words containing the syllable pattern 'is' (including prefixes like dis-, vis-, and within words like exquisite, reprisal, orison)

945.label: opening quotation mark
945.description: The feature fires on the opening double quotation mark character that begins a quoted word or phrase in dialogue or citation contexts

946.label: part/party
946.description: The feature detects the word 'part' or 'party' (and its inflections like 'particular', 'parties'), firing on the token 'part' in these substrings

947.label: noble title 'of'
947.description: The word 'of' appearing immediately after a noble or aristocratic title (Duke, Earl, Prince, Count) in a name or formal designation

948.label: mid-word token boundary
948.description: Feature fires on tokens that are mid-word fragments, particularly where a word is split across tokens (e.g., 'raz-ed', 'braz-ed', 'buzz-ed', 'coz-ened', 'eas-ily', 'eas-y', 'Proceed', 'return', 'play', 'along', 'us', 'stay')

949.label: Capital F after line break or word boundary
949.description: The feature fires on capital letter 'F' appearing at the start of a new line or after a line break, typically beginning a word like 'From', 'Fled', 'Fell', or a proper name like 'Ford' or 'Francis'

950.label: mid-word suffix fragment
950.description: Tokens that are suffixes or internal fragments of longer words, specifically the second part of a word split across tokens (e.g., 'ger' in 'Marger', 'ict' in 'Vict', 'ger' in 'swagger', 'ctor' in 'Doctor')

951.label: adverbial 'so'
951.description: The feature fires on the word 'so' used as an adverb (degree modifier, conjunctive, or interjection) in Shakespearean prose and verse contexts

952.label: Nay interjection
952.description: The feature fires on the word 'Nay' used as a spoken interjection or contradiction at the start of a character's dialogue turn in Shakespeare

953.label: I can/cannot
953.description: The feature activates on 'can' or 'cannot' when immediately preceded by the first-person pronoun 'I', capturing the specific bigram pattern 'I can' or 'I cannot' in Shakespeare's text.

954.label: dialogue turn marker
954.description: Newline or line break immediately following a period that ends a speech, preceding a new speaker tag in dramatic dialogue

955.label: r-initial syllable after line break or word boundary
955.description: The feature fires on the letter 'r' that begins a word starting with 'r' (rise, rated, rite, ransom, rotten, ranging, ranges), specifically when this 'r' follows a soft hyphenation or line-wrap boundary in the text, i.e. the 'r' is the first character of a word that has been split across a display line

956.label: letter 'g' after space/boundary
956.description: Fires on the letter 'g' at the start of a word, particularly words beginning with 'g' such as 'gave', 'girl', 'ghost', 'gentry', 'generally', 'gambol', 'gap', 'gift', 'gain', 'gazed', 'gnats'

957.label: contracted preposition 'i' th'' or 'o'er'/'o'clock'
957.description: The feature detects the apostrophe in Shakespearean contracted prepositions and phrases, specifically the elided form where a vowel is dropped and marked with an apostrophe (i' th', o'er, o'clock, o' fire), firing on the apostrophe character itself

958.label: sm/bl/cl onset
958.description: Tokens beginning with specific consonant clusters: 'sm', 'bl', or 'cl' (onset consonant clusters starting with a stop or fricative followed by a liquid or nasal)

959.label: pro- prefix
959.description: The feature activates on the token 'pro' as the beginning of words with the Latin prefix 'pro-' (proper, provoke, proclamation, prologue, process, proverbed, prodigy, prodigal, prosperous)

960.label: pronoun 'them' as object
960.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'them' when it appears as a direct or indirect object following a verb or preposition, referring back to previously mentioned plural entities

961.label: parenthetical/resumptive clause opener
961.description: Feature fires on 'As' or 'Now' when they introduce a parenthetical, aside, or resumptive clause that interrupts or qualifies the main narrative flow, often marked by dashes or embedded commentary

962.label: short utterance boundary
962.description: Fires on the newline/whitespace token immediately following a closing guillemet that ends a short spoken line or stage direction, marking the transition point between one speaker's brief utterance and the next speaker label

963.label: punctuation after clause
963.description: The feature fires on punctuation marks (colon, semicolon, comma, period, question mark) that follow a completed clause or phrase, typically marking a boundary between a statement and what follows

964.label: cont- Latin prefix morpheme
964.description: The feature activates on the internal morpheme boundary of words containing Latin-derived prefixes like 'cont-', 'prop-', where the token captures part of a prefix such as 'cont(in)', 'cont(ag)', 'prop(ag)', 'cont(am)', 'cont(r)' within longer latinate words

965.label: short dramatic utterance
965.description: Feature fires on tokens within very short speech segments or exclamations in dramatic dialogue, typically 2-5 word phrases spoken by a character

966.label: relative pronoun 'which'
966.description: The feature fires on the token 'which' (or partial token 'hich') when it functions as a relative pronoun introducing a relative clause, typically following a noun or pronoun antecedent

967.label: Latin-root words with internal morpheme boundaries
967.description: Words containing Latin-derived morphemes (counsel/consult, convent/convention, create, prevent, respect) where the feature fires on the core morpheme stem within the word

968.label: first-person plural subject 'we'
968.description: The feature activates on the token 'we' when used as a first-person plural subject pronoun, often at or near the beginning of a clause

969.label: sentence-final period after 'you'
969.description: The feature fires on periods that end clauses or sentences directed at 'you' (second person), typically following words like 'you', 'yours', or phrases addressing the interlocutor directly

970.label: interrogative or conjunctive sentence-initial word
970.description: Feature fires on 'Who' or 'And' appearing at the start of a clause or sentence, functioning as a sentence/clause-initial connective or interrogative pronoun

971.label: Capitalized 'S' starting new clause
971.description: The feature fires on a capital letter 'S' that begins a new syntactic unit (clause, sentence, or line) following a comma or period, typically introducing words like 'Save', 'South', 'Something', 'Saw', 'Show', 'Scarce', 'Sent', 'Suddenly', 'Sought', 'Send'

972.label: comm- prefix
972.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the beginning of words starting with 'comm' (communicate, commence, commit, commination, commodity, commission, communication)

973.label: discourse/response particle or proper noun mid-speech
973.description: Tokens that appear at the start of a speech turn or clause as a response/discourse marker ('Nay') or as a proper noun (country/abstract noun) following a speech verb or preposition, typically single-word pivots that redirect or anchor dialogue

974.label: letter t in apostrophe contractions
974.description: The feature fires on the letter 't' that appears as part of Early Modern English contracted verb forms (e.g., 'mak'st', 'keep'st', 'dar'st', 'stand'st', 'remember'st', 'tak'st') as well as other instances of the letter 't' mid-token

975.label: pronoun 'him' as object
975.description: The feature fires on the pronoun 'him' when used as an object (direct, indirect, or prepositional) referring to a male character previously mentioned in context

976.label: mid-word Latin/Romance fragment
976.description: The feature activates on characters within the interior of Latin or Romance-language words, particularly syllables like 'id', 'x', 'al', 'ign' appearing mid-word in Latin, French, or Latinate vocabulary

977.label: demonstrative 'This' sentence opener
977.description: The feature fires on the word 'This' when it begins a new declarative sentence or clause, typically after a period or major syntactic break, functioning as a demonstrative pronoun introducing a new statement or assertion.

978.label: dash-interrupted speech marker
978.description: Token is '.--' or similar punctuation marking an abrupt interruption or transition between speakers/clauses, specifically the closing punctuation before a double dash

979.label: mid-line poetic content words
979.description: Tokens appearing in the middle of verse lines in poetic/song passages, often within noun phrases or conjunctions in nature or seasonal imagery

980.label: concessive/contrastive discourse markers
980.description: The feature fires on 'yet' and 'then' when used as concessive or contrastive discourse markers (expressing contrast, reservation, or logical consequence) in dialogue, typically following a comma or preceding a clause that qualifies or counters the prior statement

981.label: Capital C after space or punctuation
981.description: The feature fires on the letter 'C' at the start of a proper noun (name or place), typically following a space, punctuation, or vocative context

982.label: preposition starting mid-clause
982.description: Feature fires on prepositions or conjunctions ('upon', 'half', 'whether') that appear mid-line after a caesura or syntactic break, typically introducing a dependent phrase

983.label: infinitive/purposive 'to'
983.description: The feature fires on the word 'to' when it functions as an infinitive marker or directional/purposive preposition linking a preceding noun or adjective phrase to a following verb or action

984.label: closing guillemet followed by opening guillemet
984.description: The feature fires on the closing guillemet »« pair marking a speaker turn boundary or dialogue exchange transition, specifically the token at the end of one speech and beginning of another in dramatic text

985.label: speaker tag after line break
985.description: Activates on the whitespace/newline character immediately preceding a character name/speaker label in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue format

986.label: Speaker label after line break
986.description: The feature fires on the whitespace/newline character immediately preceding a character's speech label (e.g., VIRGILIA, VALERIA, VOLUMNIA) in a dramatic script, marking the transition point between one character's speech and the next speaker's name.

987.label: mid-sentence determiner/quantifier
987.description: Feature activates on common determiners, quantifiers, or adjectives (some, the, this, these, his, own, excellent) appearing mid-sentence, often after a pause or clause boundary

988.label: he/phe syllable
988.description: Tokens containing the syllable or letters 'he' (as standalone pronoun, or embedded in words like 'prophet', 'overheard', 'rehearse', 'prophecy')

989.label: coordinating conjunction after 'our'
989.description: The feature fires on 'and' or 'than' appearing after the word 'our', typically connecting two noun phrases or clauses in a royal/plural first-person context

990.label: verb 'take'
990.description: The feature activates on the word 'take' used as a verb in various grammatical contexts across Shakespeare's works

991.label: line-ending newline token
991.description: The feature fires on the newline/line-break token that separates verse lines in Shakespeare's text, appearing at the end of a completed poetic or prose line before the next line begins

992.label: letter p after space or punctuation
992.description: The feature activates on the letter 'p' at the start of a word, particularly following a word boundary such as whitespace or punctuation (e.g., 'to «p»ore', 'to «p»unish', 'can «p»attern', 'to «p»alm')

993.label: blank line stage direction
993.description: The feature activates on the newline/whitespace token that immediately follows a closing spoken line and precedes a new speaker label (stage direction boundary between speeches)

994.label: Vocative address comma
994.description: A comma immediately following a direct address (vocative), where a character's name or title is called out followed by a comma before the rest of the speech

995.label: se- prefix fragment
995.description: The feature fires on the letters 'se' at the start of a word, capturing this two-character sequence as a token boundary fragment, appearing in words like 'severe', 'sex', 'several', 'severity', 'seventeen', 'seals', 'seest', 'search', 'seldom', 'sex', 'seething', 'seven'

996.label: ir
996.description: Detects the letter sequence 'ir' (the digraph /ɪr/ or similar), appearing mid-word in various contexts such as 'choir', 'conspire', 'admire', 'aspire', 'their', 'chair', etc.

997.label: "for" preceded by direct object/complement
997.description: The word 'for' appearing after a noun phrase or object, often in contexts of purpose, exchange, or characterization

998.label: sw- words
998.description: Tokens beginning with the letters 'sw' (sweep, swear, sweat, sway, swarm, etc.)

999.label: ous/mad suffix-final tokens
999.description: The feature activates on tokens that are the final syllable or fragment of words ending in '-ous' (heinous, ponderous, treacherous, superfluous, boisterous) or on the word 'mad' appearing as a token boundary, capturing the ending portion of polysyllabic adjectives with these suffixes

1000.label: short imperative sentence period
1000.description: Period following a short imperative or command phrase (often 2-4 words like 'Go to.', 'Come.', 'Stand aside.')

1001.label: speaker transition newline
1001.description: Activates on the newline/whitespace character immediately following a completed speech line and preceding a new speaker label in Shakespeare dramatic text

1002.label: Elided apostrophe contraction
1002.description: The feature fires on apostrophes that represent elided letters in Shakespearean contractions such as 'tis, 'tween, 'gainst, 'sayed, 'scape, 'em, 't, where the apostrophe marks the omission of an initial letter or syllable

1003.label: degree adverb in evaluative context
1003.description: The feature fires on 'much' (and occasionally 'now') when used as a degree modifier expressing extent or intensity, particularly in evaluative or emotional statements

1004.label: word 'end'
1004.description: The feature activates on the word 'end' (or the morpheme 'end' at the start of 'endurance'/'enduring'), appearing in various Shakespeare contexts

1005.label: "all" preceded by function word
1005.description: The feature fires on the word «all» when it appears immediately after a function word (conjunctions, prepositions, or relative pronouns such as 'which', 'who', 'so', 'as', 'not', 'or', 'where'), typically in constructions that introduce a sweeping or universal clause

1006.label: medial 'l' in polysyllabic words
1006.description: The feature fires on the letter 'l' appearing in the middle of longer words, most prominently in 'melancholy' and similar polysyllabic words containing an interior 'l' preceded by a vowel

1007.label: first-person possessive 'my'
1007.description: The feature activates on the word 'my' (first-person possessive pronoun) in mid-sentence contexts across Shakespeare's works

1008.label: character name label
1008.description: The feature fires on commas immediately following a character name (speaker label) in a Shakespeare play script, marking the transition from character identification to their spoken dialogue

1009.label: short function words mid-clause
1009.description: Fires on common short function words (gives, and, for, but, is, makes) appearing mid-clause, typically connecting or introducing a predicate or complement within a sentence

1010.label: preposition 'of/with' following noun phrase
1010.description: Fires on 'of' or 'with' when they appear as prepositions linking a preceding noun or adjective phrase to a following noun phrase, typically in complex nominal or participial constructions

1011.label: disjunctive 'or'
1011.description: The feature fires on the word 'or' when it functions as a disjunctive coordinating conjunction connecting two alternatives, clauses, or phrases

1012.label: interjection comma
1012.description: Comma immediately following a sentence-opening interjection or exclamation (Why, What, How, etc.) that begins a new speaker's turn

1013.label: object pronoun 'me'
1013.description: The feature fires on the token 'me' (or 'me' as part of a word like 'memory') when it appears as a direct or indirect object, typically following a verb in Shakespearean verse or prose

1014.label: Capital S after punctuation/newline
1014.description: The feature fires on the letter 'S' at the start of a new speech or sentence, particularly following a speaker label, period, or line break — i.e., a sentence-initial or utterance-initial capital S

1015.label: second person 'you are' predication
1015.description: The feature fires on 'are' immediately following 'you' (or a variant), forming a direct second-person predicate construction addressed to a specific character

1016.label: she (pronoun)
1016.description: The feature fires on the word 'she' used as a subject or object pronoun referring to a female character

1017.label: gr/tr onset cluster
1017.description: Tokens beginning with the letter cluster 'gr' or 'tr' (e.g., 'gre', 'trump', 'stre')

1018.label: ANT substring
1018.description: Fires on the token sequence 'ANT' as part of the name 'ROSENCRANTZ' or 'BRABANTIO', detecting this specific character name substring

1019.label: newline after short question
1019.description: Whitespace or newline token immediately following a short rhetorical or clarifying question (often 'What X?', 'How X?', 'What then?') posed by a character in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue

1020.label: preposition 'in'
1020.description: The feature activates on the word or fragment 'in', particularly the preposition 'in' or the 'in-' prefix in words like 'inventions', 'injustice', 'engendered in'

1021.label: ot digraph
1021.description: The feature activates on the letters 'ot' appearing mid-token, particularly in words containing the sequence 'ot' such as 'devoted', 'impotent', 'complot', 'escoted', 'plot', 'boot', 'Protector', 'commotion', 'devotion', 'afoot'

1022.label: end-of-question marker
1022.description: Feature fires on the closing guillemet/quote token that immediately follows a question mark, marking the boundary between a spoken question and the next speaker's response in Shakespeare's dramatic dialogue

1023.label: I shall / You shall
1023.description: The feature detects the word 'shall' when immediately preceded by a first-person or second-person pronoun ('I' or 'You'), capturing this specific grammatical collocation in Shakespearean dialogue.
